The proposed BGR employs current-mode proportional-to-absolute-temperature (PTAT) and complementary-to-absolute-temperature (CTAT) current generation with optimized first-order and residual second-order temperature compensation, enabling ultra-low temperature drift over a wide operating range. To achieve high power supply rejection ratio (PSRR), cascoded biasing and supply-independent current mirrors are incorporated, effectively suppressing supply noise coupling into the reference node. The circuit generates a stable reference voltage of approximately 1.2 V while consuming only a few µW of power, making it highly suitable for energy-constrained and noise-sensitive systems. Post-layout simulations in SCL 180 nm CMOS confirm a temperature coefficient as low as 0.27 ppm/°C across –40 °C to 125 °C, PSRR exceeding 150 dB at low frequencies, and robust operation under process–voltage–temperature (PVT) variations. The combination of sub-ppm/°C temperature stability, high PSRR, and ultra-low power establishes the proposed design as an efficient and reliable reference solution for advanced analog, mixed-signal, and power-management applications.

title: Real time Face Feeling Recognition using YOLO-based Face Detection and Deep Feature-Driven Machine Learning Classification
authors: Vimala P, R. Selvakani, R. Kavitha, Raaghav. R.S.T, Logeshwaran. T, Jyotheesh. S
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Expert Clouds and Applications (ICOECA)
abstract: Emotional computing, intelligent surveillance systems, and human computer interaction all depend heavily on facial emotion recognition (FER). For real time applications, however, maintaining low computational complexity while obtaining high identification accuracy continues to a major difficulty. An optimised convolutional CNN based FER framework is presented in this study along with a performance analysis that compares it to other approaches, such as lightweight CNNs, landmark based attention models, transformer based FER architectures, and hybrid deep feature methods. Through effective parameter optimisation and task specific architectural modification, the suggested optimised CNN architecture improves discriminative feature learning. The suggested process outperforms state of FER techniques transversely conventional assessment measures. The robustness of the suggested approach in reducing misclassification mistakes is confirmed by the balanced improvement in precision and recall. These findings suggest that effective trade off between computational efficiency then recognition presentation can be achieved with optimised CNN-based designs. As a result, the suggested FER architecture is a viable option for useful affect-aware intelligent systems since it works well in real time and resource-constrained settings.

title: An Inductive Wireless Power Transfer System with Frequency Domain Analysis and Photovoltaic Design in Electric Vehicles
authors: G. Ramkumar
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Expert Clouds and Applications (ICOECA)
abstract: Recharging an Electric Vehicle (EV) battery with electricity that has been "put back" into the grid is essentially replenishing the battery with power, which is most of the time done by means of cables or through systems that allow power to be sent without wires. We came up with an inductive wireless power and feedback-driven photovoltaic (IWPFDP) system that facilitates renewable energy-based wireless EV charging to be more efficient. The unit generates power through a Photovoltaic (PV) module that is made energy-efficient by using a Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) method that takes different environmental factors into account. A high- frequency inverter changes the direct current output for wireless sending to another coil by using inductive power transfer. This setup implements an Active Load Impedance Matching (ALIM) scheme to power flow more efficiently and lessen the conversion losses. Balanced pulsed-width modulation makes sure charging stays steady, even when load conditions change. The performance metrics are efficiency, voltage & power, power loss and load. It achieves 70% at 250 W to 80% at 700W, over 77% at 550W, where the 310V range achieves 80V at 9A, and decreases by 40V at 1A, later 650W to 800W was achieved in 250 mins duration, the output from 262W to 725W we achieve 264.9W in HF - inerter of (149.5W, in 56%), IPT of (99.1W, in 37%), and HF - rectifier of (16.3W, in 6%).

title: High Performance Slotted Rectangular Textile UWB Antenna With Modified CPW Configuration for Wban Applications
authors: Nanditha. D, Mohan. C, Vidhushi. B
publish: 2026 Twelfth International Conference on Bio Signals, Images, and Instrumentation (ICBSII)
abstract: This work describes a small Ultra-Wideband (UWB) antenna constructed on a cotton fabric substrate for usage in wearable and body-centric wireless systems. To enhance impedance characteristics and bandwidth, the antenna uses a redesigned radiating patch with square, semicircular, and rectangular parts as well as carefully positioned horizontal and vertical slots. To enable flexible integration on textile materials, a CPW-fed architecture is used. With a reflection coefficient of less than - 10 dB, the optimized construction achieves a broad operating range from 3 GHz to 10.8 GHz. The design retains radiation efficiency beyond 81% and offers stable gain levels between 2.5 and 3.4 dB despite its small dimension of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$17 \times 17 \times 1 ~\text{mm}^{3}$</tex>. These findings demonstrate the antenna's applicability for wearable and implanted WBAN applications, when wideband performance, comfort, and compactness are significant.

title: Inverse Design of Photonic Crystal Lasers Through Automatic Differentiation Optimization
authors: Mihai Crisan, Shamsul Arafin
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics
abstract: The behavior of (embedded) PhC resonator lasers is inextricably linked to the unit cell profile. Hence, an overwhelming fraction of the design process unfortunately relies on the judicious manual choice of the permittivity profile. This is disadvantageous for the fact that this throws away information regarding the objective function (the physical response of the laser). We remedy this by employing gradient-based protocols that split the computational design task into two steps—between a 2D eigenvalue problem for the photonic eigenmodes (modeling) and the computational graph (gradient-propagation): (i) a forward passage through the simulation to predict the photonic-crystal surface-emitting laser (PCSEL) behavior (far-field and mode quality factors), and (ii) a backpropagation step where the gradient of a (user) defined cost function is computed to update the unit cell design. The performance of this method exceeds contemporary unit cell designs for large-scale PCSELs in both far-field and intracavity field performance metrics.

title: Test Time Optimization for Highly-Reliable Redundant Systems with Single-machine Similarity and Non-Uniform Labor-hours
authors: Chengjie Zhang, Zhigeng Fang, Rui Shi, Youpeng Liu, Zhongwen Chen
abstract: Reliability demonstration of highly-reliable redundant systems traditionally requires large-sample, long-duration tests, resulting in prohibitively lengthy test cycles and excessive resource consumption. However, existing approaches have not fully exploited system redundancy structure and similarity to reduce test time. To address this gap, this paper integrates redundancy structure, single-machine similarity, and non-uniform labor-hour information into a system-level test time minimization framework. First, an analytical expression for test time is derived by leveraging the sample-size expansion effect of redundant structures. Second, a similarity conversion coefficient is defined to incorporate single-machine similarity into the optimization model. Third, Bayesian methods are utilized to integrate prior test information. Through system-level optimization that optimally allocates reliability targets to individual units, this work achieves joint optimization of redundancy, similarity, and prior information for the first time. A case study demonstrates that the proposed approach reduces test time by up to 74% compared with conventional methods that use similarity solely for prior construction. This study provides an efficient solution for rapid reliability verification of aerospace systems, and the proposed framework is applicable to other exponential lifetime systems with redundant architectures.

title: Reliable and Compact Graph Fine-Tuning Via Graph Sparse Prompting
authors: Bo Jiang, Hao Wu, Beibei Wang, Jin Tang, Bin Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Recently, graph prompt learning has garnered increasing attention in adapting pre-trained GNN models for downstream graph learning tasks. However, existing works generally conduct prompting over all graph elements (e.g., nodes, edges, node attributes, etc.), which is suboptimal and obviously redundant. To address this issue, we propose exploiting sparse representation theory for graph prompting and present Graph Sparse Prompting (GSP). GSP aims to adaptively and sparsely select the optimal elements (e.g., certain node attributes) to achieve compact prompting for downstream tasks. Specifically, we propose two kinds of GSP models, termed Graph Sparse Feature Prompting (GSFP) and Graph Sparse multi-Feature Prompting (GSmFP). Both GSFP and GSmFP provide a general scheme for tuning any specific pre-trained GNNs that can select some desired attributes for prompting by employing sparsity-guided prompt learning. A simple yet effective algorithm has been designed for solving GSFP and GSmFP models. Experiments on 16 widely-used benchmark datasets validate the effectiveness and advantages of the proposed GSFPs. Our code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/wuhaoxz/GSFP</uri>.

title: Magnetic Hyperthermia Based on Hysteresis Heating Alone
authors: Kevin O’Grady, Vijay Patel, Iestyn Jones, Gonzalo Vallejo-Fernandez
abstract: In this work we describe a new approach to magnetic hyperthermia based on hysteresis alone. This is achieved by encapsulating magnetite nanoparticles in polymer spheres to remove dynamic effects which cause unpredictable and uncontrollable heating. This also prevents aggregation of the nanoparticles. To generate significant hysteresis and hence heat, we have used nanoparticles grown by the standard coprecipitation process which are used as seeds and grown to a larger size by the addition of further Fe<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2+</sup> salts in an alkaline solution and heating. We define this as a Controlled Growth Process (CGP). The CGP particles are found to have a much higher saturation magnetisation than particles produced by coprecipitation enhancing their magnetic shape anisotropy. Via magnetic measurements and complementary studies of the heat generated when the polymer spheres are exposed to an ac field, we show that the heat generated is due to hysteresis losses with potentially residual contributions from dynamic effects. At 111 kHz and a maximum applied field of 180 Oe, a specific absorption rate (SAR) value of ∼35 W/gFe was determined from calorimetry measurements. This value agrees within error with the losses calculated from the hysteresis loop. When the same particles are not encapsulated, significant viscous heating is present.

title: WordCon: Word-level Typography Control in Visual Text Rendering
authors: Wenda Shi, Yiren Song, Zihan Rao, Dengming Zhang, Jiaming Liu, Xingxing Zou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Visual text rendering represents a fundamental capability of large-scale text-to-image (T2I) models, yet achieving precise word-level controllability remains a significant challenge in this domain. While existing approaches primarily focus on text content accuracy, they often fail to provide fine-grained control over typographic attributes at the word level. To address this limitation, we introduce a comprehensive solution comprising three key components: (1) a novel word-level controlled scene text dataset and benchmark, (2) the Text-Image Alignment (TIA) framework that leverages cross-modal correspondence between textual queries and local image regions through grounding models, and (3) WordCon, a hybrid parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) method that employs selective parameter reparameterization to enhance both computational efficiency and model portability. The proposed framework incorporates additive supervision mechanisms: a masked loss at the latent level to focus on text regions, and a joint-attention loss at the feature level to promote disentanglement between different words. Extensive experimental evaluations demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both qualitative and quantitative metrics. The proposed method exhibits remarkable versatility, enabling seamless integration with diverse pipelines, including artistic text rendering and image-conditioned text generation. Our datasets, source code, and models will be available for academic research.

title: Spoofing Detection and Mitigation in GNSS Using Cyclostationarity and Sparse Array
authors: Mojtaba Khaki, Mohamad Farzan Sabahi, Saeed Gazor, Zhang Zhenkai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Spoofing attacks (SAs) in Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) involve transmitting counterfeit timing and location information and pose serious threats to many critical infrastructures. Target GNSS receivers are vulnerable to lowpower SAs, which may lead to unavailability, navigation errors, or entirely spurious position/time solutions. Traditional SA detectors typically assume a single SA originating from a specific direction with higher power than the authentic signals. These methods rely on eigendecomposition of the spatial covariance matrix to identify the spoofer's direction and often fail to detect lowpower spoofers or multiple simultaneous spoofers. In this paper, we introduce a spatio-temporal method to distinguish spoofers from authentic signals using a sparse array antenna in the pre-correlation stage of the GNSS receiver. We employ high-resolution direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimation followed by a power-independent cyclostationarity detector. For each direction, the received signal consists of either a single authentic satellite signal or multiple counterfeit (spoofer) signals. The proposed detector decides between these two hypotheses by estimating the number of impinging signals, and we construct an asymptotically optimal detector using a suitable separating function. In addition, we propose a spatial filter to obtain a spooferfree signal, thereby enhancing the robustness of GNSS receivers against lowpower and multiSAs. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed sparsearray approach in separating and localizing multiple individual spoofers—even in multipath environments—each transmitting multiple counterfeit signals.

title: Inferring Spatial Reasoning by Observing Human Interactions With Smart IoT Objects
authors: Subir Biswas, Amit Kumar Bhuyan, Hrishikesh Dutta, Saptarshi Das, Amy Noelle Parks, Kiley McKee, Danielle Rothschild Doyle, David H Uttal
abstract: This paper presents a proof-of-concept for a new paradigm for analyzing human-object interactions with high spatiotemporal resolution. The system comprises a set of smart IoT objects, equipped with sensors to monitor their spatial states and surroundings, including other smart objects. Each object is able to detect, store, and wirelessly report its orientation, rotation, adjacency, and various types of movements, both its own and those of neighboring objects. By collating this data, an analytical model can reconstruct a participant’s spatiotemporal handling of the objects with millisecond resolution, thereby inferring their spatial reasoning process. The objective is to establish a IoT echo systems for monitoring, characterizing, and enhancing human spatial reasoning through this novel approach of observing interactions between humans and objects with agency. The paper delves into the engineering details of the IoT system, followed by a series of spatial reasoning-related experiments. Subsequently, analytical methods are developed to gain insights into participants’ spatial reasoning within the context of two well-known tasks: the Tower of Hanoi and the Wechsler Block Design Task. Finally, the results are presented to demonstrate the practicality of the developed IoT-style smart object system for quantitatively understanding spatial reasoning in a data-driven manner.

title: Application of IEEE Std. 1459-2010 and Hjorth Descriptors for the Identification of Stator Faults in Induction Motors
authors: Arthur C. Santos, Marcelo F. Castoldi, Wesley A. Souza, Alessandro Goedtel
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Industrial Electronics Society
abstract: Stator interturn short-circuit (ITSC) faults represent a significant fraction of failures in three-phase induction motors (TIMs), with industrial studies reporting occurrences of up to 36%. Current-based diagnostic techniques are widely employed; however, their effectiveness degrades under inherent supply asymmetries, manufacturing tolerances, and moderate voltage imbalances, which introduce negative-sequence and harmonic components that mask incipient fault signatures and can produce current components similar to those generated by internal electromagnetic asymmetries. Diagnostic capability is investigated using two families of features extracted from stator electrical signals: power quantities defined by IEEE Std. 1459-2010 and Hjorth time-domain descriptors. These feature families correspond to distinct physical manifestations observable in the measured currents: macroscopic redistribution of electrical power under nonsinusoidal and unbalanced operating conditions, and local variations in waveform geometry associated with circulating currents and early flux asymmetries. Model performance is evaluated through stratified <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math></inline-formula>-fold cross-validation using five supervised classifiers applied to a labeled laboratory dataset comprising multiple ITSC severities (healthy, 1%, 3%, 5%, and 10%), controlled voltage unbalance, multiple mechanical loading conditions, and supply-frequency variation. Hjorth parameters present higher sensitivity to low-severity faults and achieve classification accuracies above 99%, whereas IEEE Std. 1459-2010 quantities yield accuracies between 78% and 95%, reflecting reduced sensitivity to weak distortions but intrinsically phase-invariant behavior. A reduced hybrid feature set combining selected IEEE Std. 1459-2010 descriptors with the Hjorth parameters Activity and Mobility reduces phase dependence and produces classification accuracies exceeding 99.5% across all evaluated classifiers. Correlation analysis and feature-importance rankings indicate limited redundancy between descriptor groups and reveal complementary diagnostic information associated with electrical power redistribution and waveform-geometry variations. The obtained results indicate that early-stage ITSC faults are identifiable using a compact descriptor space derived from physically interpretable electrical quantities, without dependence on high-dimensional time-frequency representations or deep learning models.

title: Online Time-Series Contrastive Learning for Soft Sensing of Biopharmaceutical Processes_supp1-3679614.pdf
authors: Xinggao Liu
abstract: Biopharmaceutical processes typically generate abundant high-frequency online sensor data but suffer from sparse and delayed offline quality measurements, creating a “data-rich but label-poor” dilemma that hinders effective process monitoring. Furthermore, these processes are subject to significant distribution shifts due to batch-to-batch variability and time-varying metabolic states. To address these challenges, this article proposes a novel online time-series contrastive learning framework for soft sensing, validated on an industrial erythromycin fermentation process. We introduce a mechanism-informed soft contrastive learning strategy that utilizes information derived from domain knowledge to construct instance-level and temporal similarity matrices, guiding the model to learn physically meaningful representations from unlabeled data. In addition, we develop a dual-timescale online learning architecture comprising a slow branch for robust representation learning and a fast branch for real-time adaptation. Experimental results on a large-scale industrial dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art time-series contrastive learning baselines, particularly in predicting complex rheological indicators like broth viscosity under temporal distribution shifts.

title: WaveMambaFormer: A Hybrid Network for Global Significant Wave Height Retrieval Based on Tianmu-1 Constellation GNSS-R Data With Different Polarizations
authors: Jinwei Bu, Chaoying Ji, Kegen Yu, Feixiong Huang, Huan Li, Xinyu Liu, Qiulan Wang, Yutong Wang, Xiaoqing Zuo
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The combination of Global Navigation Satellite System-Reflectometry (GNSS-R) Measurement technology and deep learning models has shown significant advantages in the research of Significant Wave Height (SWH) retrieval. Given that different polarization characteristics have different performances for sea level monitoring, this paper proposes for the first time a hybrid deep learning model (named WaveMambaFormer) that integrates Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Transformer, and State Space Model (SSM) based on GPS-R/BDS-R/GLONASS-R/Galileo-R polarization mode data from the Tianmu-1 satellite, to retrieve SWH in high sea conditions (0-12 m). Widely test the retrieval results by using multiple reference data. Research has found that circular polarization (RL/RR) observations perform better in retrieving SWH, with correlation coefficient (CC) values maintained above 0.88. Linear polarization (RH/RV) observations perform even worse, especially in RV observations, with a maximum CC of only 0.50, making them unsuitable for SWH retrieval. In addition, we used Tianmu-1 four systems (BDS/GPS/Galileo and GLONASS) single polarization satellite data to retrieve SWH separately, and compared the WaveMambaFormer model with existing models. When using ERA5 as a reference, the RMSE of the WaveMambaFormer model for retrieving SWH in the 0-12 m range was 0.558 -0.600 m, and the R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> was 0.87-0.89. Specifically, compared with previous models, it performed the best in the 4-8 m and 8-12 m SWH ranges, with RMSE improvements of at least 7.06% and 14.35%, respectively. Finally, we conducted a visual analysis of the input variables for the WaveMambaFormer model using the SHAP interpreter.

title: Remote Sensing Monitoring of Glacier Changes in the Puruogangri Glacier Over the Past Four Decades
authors: Boao Li, Xiaoyan Wang, Shi Liang, Hao Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The Puruogangri Glacier represents the largest glacier system on the central Tibetan Plateau. Existing glacier-identification algorithms are often susceptible to cloud contamination and frequently underutilize cloud-free pixels, leading to considerable uncertainties in extraction results. The Snow Cover Occurrence Index (SCOI) based method reduces cloud effects and seasonal snow, providing reliable glacier information for long-term change analysis. In this study, 2300 Landsat images from 1986 to 2024 are used in the SCOI method to extract the glacier boundaries of the Puruogangri Glacier over nearly four decades. Results show an overall glacier retreat from 1986 to 2024, with area decreasing from 416.22 ± 9.84 km<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> to 380.66 ± 8.99 km<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, a total loss of 35.56 km<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> (0.24% a<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>). The most significant glacier recession occurred during 1988–1994 (0.58% a<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>) and 1997–2003 (0.57% a<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>). In contrast, a slight glacier expansion was observed between 1994 and 1997 (0.85% a<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>). Glacier changes were primarily concentrated in glacier-terminus regions at elevations below 5500 m. Among eight glaciers exhibiting notable changes, seven underwent continuous retreat, while one advanced slightly. During the same period, both temperature and precipitation in the region displayed increasing trends. Glacier area change correlated negatively with both temperature and precipitation, with a stronger correlation observed for temperature.

title: An Efficient Cross-Agent Spatial-Temporal Collaboration Framework for Environmental Perception in IoV
authors: Yang Li, Quan Yuan, Xiaoyuan Fu, Guiyang Luo, Yujia Yang, Jiawen Kang, Jinglin Li, Dusit Niyato
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: By sharing complementary perceptual information, cross-agent collaborative perception fosters a deeper understanding of the environment. Recent studies mostly utilize CNN or Transformer structures to learn feature representation during feature encoding and fusion, which struggle to handle long-range spatial-temporal features under limited computing and communication resources, resulting in suboptimal perception performance. Holistically modeling the dependencies over extensive spatial areas and extended temporal cues is crucial to enhancing feature quality. To this end, we propose CollaMamba, a resource-efficient cross-agent spatial-temporal collaboration framework. We first optimize feature encoding and fusion pipeline based on the spatial state space model, which effectively captures spatial causal dependencies from both single-agent and cross-agent perspectives, yielding compact and comprehensive intermediate features while maintaining linear complexity. Subsequently, to further improve feature encoding quality, we devise a history-aware feature boosting component that extracts contextual cues from extended historical frames, refining ambiguous features while maintaining low overhead. To ensure the reliability of feature fusion under unstable communication conditions, we develop a cross-agent collaborative prediction component capable of delivering reliable perception results even when connections to neighboring agents are inaccessible. Extensive experiments across several datasets demonstrate that CollaMamba outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving higher model accuracy while reducing computational and communication overhead by up to 71.9% and 98.2%, respectively. This work pioneers the exploration of the Mamba’s potential in collaborative perception.

title: Hierarchical Optimization for Time-Varying Resource Allocation of Networked Uncertain Robotic Agents With Inconstant Parameters
authors: Zi-Heng Yi, Ming-Feng Ge, Teng-Fei Ding, Zhi-Wei Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal and Information Processing over Networks
abstract: The existing time-invariant resource allocation methods for general multi-agent systems are difficult to cope with dynamic and time-varying practical application scenarios such as unmanned ocean exploration and ground disaster rescue. Therefore, there is an urgent need for a reliable and safe strategy to meet the optimal distributed time-varying resource allocation (DTVRA) requirements of multi-robot systems in this context. This paper investigates the DTVRA problem of networked uncertain robotic agents (NURAs) with inconstant parameters. We propose a novel hierarchical optimization strategy including the layers of a distributed resource-optimization-based (DROB) estimator and a local adaptive controller. Specifically, the tools of Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions and gradient descent are used to construct a distributed Lagrange multiplier subsystem in the estimator layer, guaranteeing that the estimator states converge to the optimal solutions of DTVRA problem. Furthermore, only the Lagrange multipliers and their derivatives are exchanged instead of the physical states through the interaction topology to address the DTVRA problem in a distributed manner. In addition, a local adaptive controller is constructed so that the physical states of NURAs can be driven to track the estimated values obtained by the DROB estimator, such that the global cost function is minimized under the constraints of the time-varying resources. By employing Lyapunov stability theory, several sufficient conditions on the convergence of the closed-loop system are formally derived. Finally, the simulation examples are carried out to verify the performance of the designed hierarchical optimization algorithm.

title: Auto-Weighted Multiple Graph Learning from Smooth Signals Across Data Silos_supp1-3683223.pdf
authors: Xiang Zhang
abstract: We consider the problem of inferring graph topology from smooth graph signals in a novel but practical scenario where data are located in multiple clients and prohibited from leaving local clients due to factors such as privacy concerns. The main difficulty in this task is how to exploit the potentially heterogeneous data of all clients under data silos. To this end, we first propose an auto-weighted multiple graph learning model to jointly learn a personalized graph for each local client and a single consensus graph for all clients. The personalized graphs match local data distributions, thereby mitigating data heterogeneity, while the consensus graph captures the global information. Moreover, the model can automatically assign appropriate contribution weights to local graphs based on their similarity to the consensus graph. We next devise a tailored algorithm to solve the induced problem, where all raw data are processed locally without leaving clients. Theoretically, we establish a provable estimation error bound and convergence analysis for the proposed model and algorithm. Finally, extensive experiments on synthetic and real data are carried out, and the results illustrate that our approach can learn graphs effectively in the target scenario.

title: Editorial
authors: Issouf Fofana
abstract: Through my involvement with the IEEE Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation Society (DEIS), I have seen firsthand how dielectrics and insulating materials—the backbone of modern power systems—ensure safety, reliability, and efficiency across high-voltage transformers, cables, gas-insulated systems, power electronics, renewable integration, and electrified transport. DEIS's conferences, workshops, standards development, publications, and collaborative research address pressing challenges—asset aging, reliability, sustainability, and technology integration—shaping international best practices and guiding utilities and manufacturers worldwide.

title: Multi-Scale Spatial-Temporal Representation Learning for mmWave Radar 3D Human Pose Estimation
authors: Yaxin Li, Jun Wang, Changshun Yuan, Xiaoming Yuan, Yuquan Luo, Song Liang
abstract: Millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar has emerged as a promising sensing modality for 3D human pose estimation in ubiquitous Internet of Things (IoT) applications, owing to its privacy-preserving nature and robustness under challenging illumination conditions. However, accurate skeletal reconstruction remains difficult due to the inherent sparsity, non-uniform distribution, and instability of radar point clouds. To address these challenges, this paper proposes MS-STPoseNet, a unified multi-scale spatio-temporal learning framework for robust mmWavebased human pose estimation. For spatial representation, MS-STPoseNet employs a hierarchical multi-scale spatial encoder built upon PointNet++, which leverages multi-scale grouping to effectively capture body structures across different spatial resolutions, from fine-grained joint regions to limb- and torso-level configurations, under irregular radar observations. For temporal modeling, a multi-branch Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) with different dilation rates is introduced to model multi-rate motion dynamics, enabling effective representation of both rapid limb movements and smoother torso motions. An attention mechanism is further incorporated to enhance informative temporal features while suppressing noise. The entire framework is trained end-to-end to estimate per-frame 3D poses by exploiting short-term temporal context, thereby improving robustness under noisy sensing conditions. Extensive experiments conducted on a self-collected dataset and two public benchmarks demonstrate that MS-STPoseNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of pose estimation accuracy and cross-subject generalization, achieving an MPJPE of 3.08 cm on the self-collected dataset. In addition, the proposed framework exhibits favorable computational efficiency and a compact model size, highlighting its potential applicability to practical IoT sensing systems.

title: Topology-Optimized, Dual-Phase Gripper with Force Estimation for Underwater Operation_supp1-3683323.mp4
authors: Chaoyang Song

title: GATE: Graph and Text Exchange for Zero-Shot ECG Classification with LLM Prompts
authors: Ying An, Shiyu Tang, Xianlai Chen, Lin Guo
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Electrocardiography (ECG) is a fundamental tool for diagnosing cardiovascular diseases, yet the scarcity of large-scale annotated data limits the applicability of supervised learning approaches. While self-supervised learning (SSL) has shown promise for ECG representation learning, existing methods often suffer from semantic distortion, insufficient spatial modeling, and a lack of integration with medical knowledge. To address these challenges, we propose GATE (Graph-And-Text Exchange), a novel multimodal SSL framework that enhances the quality of the representation of ECG through cross-modal exchange between graph-structured data and clinical ECG reports. GATE employs a spatiotemporal graph encoder to capture fine-grained intra- and inter-lead dependencies, and introduces a lexical knowledge-embedded codebook to enhance the semantic representation of clinical reports, facilitating effective graph-text alignment. During inference, GATE integrates a large language model with a domain-specific knowledge base to generate semantically enriched disease descriptions, enabling robust zero-shot classification. Extensive experiments on three real-world ECG datasets demonstrate that GATE outperforms state-of-the-art self-supervised and multimodal baselines under both low-resource and zero-shot settings. Notably, GATE achieves competitive performance even when trained on only 1% of labeled data, highlighting its strong generalization and clinical potential.

title: MLRUI-R: Multilabel Feature Selection Considering Relative Uncertainty Information
authors: Weiyi Huang, Xin Lu, Jianhua Dai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: Granular computing, which simulates human cognition by partitioning objects into multiple granules, serves as a valuable approach for handling data with uncertainty and has been widely applied to multilabel feature selection. However, existing methods based on granular computing typically assume equal importance across all labels, which limits their ability to capture the relative recognition of features within the label space. In this article, we address this limitation by analyzing label space dependencies through the lens of rough set theory. In addition, a novel method for multilabel feature selection is introduced, which incorporates relative uncertainty information. First, we propose an object grid-based acceleration method to speed up the computation of relationships in the process of constructing neighborhood granularity, and provide a new definition of neighborhood granularity on this basis. Second, based on the constructed neighborhood granularity, we define uncertainty measures under multilabel data and analyze their corresponding theoretical properties. Finally, by analyzing the dependencies within the label space, we derive an importance matrix for the labels and, by combining it with the defined uncertainty measure, develop a multilabel feature selection method that incorporates relative uncertainty. The experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Delay-Doppler Domain Channel Measurements and Modeling in High-Speed Railways
authors: Hao Zhou, Yiyan Ma, Dan Fei, Weirong Liu, Zhengyu Zhang, Mi Yang, Guoyu Ma, Yunlong Lu, Ruisi He, Guoyu Wang, Cheng Li, Zhaohui Song, Bo Ai
abstract: As next-generation wireless communication systems need to be able to operate in high-frequency bands and high-mobility scenarios, delay-Doppler (DD) domain multicarrier (DDMC) modulation schemes, such as orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS), demonstrate superior reliability over orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM). Accurate DD domain channel modeling is essential for DDMC system design. However, since traditional channel modeling approaches are mainly confined to time, frequency, and space domains, the principles of DD domain channel modeling remain poorly studied. To address this issue, we propose a systematic DD domain channel measurement and modeling methodology in high-speed railway (HSR) scenarios. First, we design a DD domain channel measurement method based on the long-term evolution for railway (LTE-R) system. Second, for DD domain channel modeling, we investigate quasi-stationary interval, statistical power modeling of multipath components, and particularly, the quasi-invariant intervals of DD domain channel fading coefficients. Third, via LTE-R measurements at 371 km/h, taking the quasi-stationary interval as the decision criterion, we establish DD domain channel models under different channel time-varying conditions in HSR scenarios. Fourth, the accuracy of proposed DD domain channel models is validated via bit error rate comparison of OTFS transmission. In addition, simulation verifies that in HSR scenario, the quasi-invariant interval of DD domain channel fading coefficient is on millisecond (ms) order of magnitude, which is much smaller than the quasi-stationary interval length on 100 ms order of magnitude. This study could provide theoretical guidance for DD domain modeling in high-mobility environments, supporting future DDMC and integrated sensing and communication designs for 6G and beyond.
