title: Source Code Metrics based bug severity prediction using PCA and RFE
authors: Sweety Ahlawat, Dhiraj Khurana
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Advancing Sustainable Solutions through Technologies (ICASST)
abstract: Predicting bug severity is a crucial aspect of software maintenance. Over recent decades, significant research has been dedicated to this area, mostly focus on bug reports description and various source code metrics. In this study, identify the most relevant metrics for predicting bug severity using ‘Principal Component Analysis (PCA)’ and ‘Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE)’. Two datasets were employed for our experiments. Employed six machine learning models and accuracy, recall, and F1-score metrics were used to evaluate the efficiency of ‘Naïve Bayes (NB), Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), AdaBoost, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Decision Tree (DT). Random Forest (RF)’ surpasses other models in bug severity classification with the greatest F1-score (64.56%), Precision (65.72%), and Recall (63.44%) with RFE. With F1-scores of 50.05% and 51.69%, KNN and DT balance Precision and Recall quite well. Naïve Bayes (NB) and AdaBoost have low predictive power, with NB scoring 43.63% and AdaBoost 52.81%, indicating inefficiency in problem severity categorization. SVM's performance shows an exceptionally high F1-score (65.01%) despite weak Precision (32.61%) and Recall (34.03%), suggesting reporting or calculation errors. RF is the most accurate model for software issue severity prediction, balancing Precision and Recall across both feature selection methods.

title: Integrating Cognitive Science and AI for Skin Disorder Detection
authors: Anagha Kulkarni, Priyanka Pawar, Bhavana Pansare, Harshal Raje, Rani Patil
publish: 2026 Innovations in Machine, Engineering, and Digital Conference (IMED)
abstract: Dermatological diagnosis has faith in heavily on expert visual interpretation nevertheless remains vulnerable to variability arising from inconsistent imaging conditions, inadequate specialist availability, and subjective assessment. To incorporate these challenges, this paper introduces CogDermNet, a conceptual framework for real-time diagnostic support that incorporates cognitive science principles with artificial intelligence for detection of various types of skin disorders. The presented architecture incorporates cognitive visual awareness, memory-augmented reasoning, and multimodal contextual incorporation to mimic human perceptual and analytical activities. CogDermNet is aimed to process dermoscopic and clinical images acquired in various real-world sceneries and to produce interpretable predictions accompanied by human-associated justifications. Even though the system has not yet endured clinical or experimental estimation, this case study explains its potential applicability in primary care triage, remote dermatology, and telemedicine systems. By grounding AI components in cognitive procedures, CogDermNet seeks to enhance transparency, decrease diagnostic vagueness, and support trustworthy decision-making in forthcoming dermatology applications.

title: A Sequential CNN-based Approach for Parkinson’s Disease Assessment and Rehabilitation using Handwriting
authors: Sujatha R, Koushika R M, Lokitha P P, Keerthika S, Indumathi R
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Expert Clouds and Applications (ICOECA)
abstract: Parkinson’s disease is a neurological disorder that leads to the loss of motor, micrograpia, and other fine motor skills. As the loss of handwriting ability is one of the first symptoms of Parkinson’s disease, it can be used as an indicator for early detection and tracking. In this paper, we propose a sequential Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) approach for the assessment of Parkinson’s disease using handwriting analysis. The handwriting samples are taken after a controlled writing task and analyzed to extract spatial and sequential information that would thus reveal motor dysfunction. This CNN methodologies is mainly developed to classify the disease by detecting the corresponding changes in handwriting patterns through deep feature extraction. Based on the experiments conducted on the dataset collected, the proposed approach is able to achieve a classification accuracy of 97.2%, thus successfully differentiating between Parkinson’s disease, affected handwriting, and healthy handwriting. Furthermore, the differences in CNN, based on confidence levels calculated from various stages of assessment, are taken into account for rehabilitation, tracking, and motor performance assessment. The results of the research have proved that the handwriting-based sequential CNN approach could be a trustworthy, non-invasive, and objective method for early detection and continuous monitoring of Parkinson’s disease, which has practical applications in the field of medicine.

title: A Flexible FPGA Approach to Image Cryptography using PYNQ-Z2
authors: Lagieni Sreenithai, Raghavendra SM, Chandrakiran SM, Mulukuri Vallesh Nath, Chaithra S
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Communication Networks and Computing (CNC)
abstract: As multimedia data transmission has experienced a sudden explosion, ensuring safe image transfer has become an essential challenge, particularly in medical imaging, surveillance, and IoT devices. This paper introduces a reconfigurable FPGA-based image cryptography scheme based on the PYNQ-Z2 development board to provide real-time image encryption and decryption. The system under development divides input images into blocks, performs a user-specified encryption algorithm generated through Vivado HLS synthesis, and processes them on the FPGA fabric for high-speed performance. Pre- and post-image processing, data transfer, and visualization are handled by a software interface based on Python, allowing hassle-free communication between the Processing System (PS) and Programmable Logic (PL) through AXI DMA. Experimental validation confirms sub-second encryption and decryption latency, substantial throughput boost over software-based approaches, and minimal power use. Scalability, extensibility, and future potential to integrate with AI-powered diagnostic and analysis pipelines are provided by the modular architecture, so this method is particularly suitable for secure image transmission in latency-sensitive and resource-limited environments.

title: Secure Artificial Intelligence-Driven Predictive Analytics for Optimizing Hospital Operations and Resource Utilization
authors: S.Anuja, Sandeep Kumar Thota, R Archana Reddy, Gourav Bathla, Anandakumar Haldorai, S.Mathumohan
publish: 2026 International Conference on ICT and Photonics (ICTP)
abstract: This study proposes a secure AI-driven predictive analytics framework to optimise the operation of the hospital and the resource utilisation. The framework makes use of multi-modal hospital data such as electronic health records, real-time sensor data and staffing schedules to predict patient inflow, occupancy of ICU beds and resource requirements. A hybrid approach using deep learning techniques (LSTM, Transformer) and classical machine learning models (Gradient Boosting) are used for the accurate prediction of data and privacy-preserving techniques like federated learning and differential privacy are used for ensuring data confidentiality. The framework has also added a multi-objective optimization layer for the efficient allocation of resources and a real-time decision support dashboard for the administrators. Experimental validation using existing data on the MIMIC-IV data set show superior predicting ability over existing state-of-the-art models. The results of our study emphasize the opportunities of integrating AI-based predictive analytics and robust security procedures to increase operational efficiency, improve patient care and hospital resource management in contemporary healthcare systems.

title: Simulation-based Investigation of Voltage Gain and Ripple in a Single-Switch DC–DC Converter
authors: Pushpalatha D, Arthi M, Divyadharshini D, Ajitha M, Deepika S
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Expert Clouds and Applications (ICOECA)
abstract: In the current system of power electronics, high gain DC-DC converters are necessary in a task that involves efficient voltage step-up and simple complex component requirements. In this paper, a single switch quartic boost converter with a switched voltage multiplier (SVM) was implemented in order to obtain higher voltage gain with low output ripple. The suggested topology utilizes the energy transfer based on the use of diodes, passive filters, and pulse-width modulation (PWM) to control the output voltage at different load and duty cycle conditions. The simulations are carried out in MATLAB/Simulink, to determine voltage gain, transient response, steady-state behavior and continuity conduction mode (CCM) operation. Findings have shown that the converter has a high voltage amplification and low ripple as well as stable dynamic response over a wide operating range. The SVM with the gain-integration allows an enhancement gain but without significant stress at switching, to which the design is applicable to renewable energy interfaces, battery-powered devices and low-power electronic systems. The simulation framework developed also offers a good tool in testing control techniques before hardware implementation.

title: A Hybrid Deep Learning Approach for Automated Bone Joint Fracture Identification and Localization
authors: Atul Kumar, Harsh Sharma, Vivek Kumar, Uma Shankar Yadav, Ashish Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Communication Networks and Computing (CNC)
abstract: Effective treatment of bone joint fractures depends on accurate and fast diagnosis of the bone joint fracture. Even though radiographic imaging is the commonly used diagnostic technique, manual clinician interpretation is time-consuming and prone to inter-observer variation. This study suggests a hybrid deep learning system that can automate the process of bone joint fracture identification and localization on radiographic images. The model has integrated a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to extract features the best, a LightGBM classifier to predict the fracture type, and the YOLOv8 object detector to localize the fracture region. The dataset used to evaluate our proposed framework is a publicly available musculoskeletal radiograph dataset. The experimental results reveal that the hybrid model is classified with an accuracy of 95.7 percent and the average Precision (mAP@0.5) of 92.3 percent in the localization task; thus, the hybrid model can be a viable computer-aided diagnostic tool, which can be implemented in clinical practice.

title: Dolphin Echolocation-Inspired Energy-Efficient Routing for Sustainable Marine Ecosystem Monitoring in Underwater Sensor Networks
authors: Deepali Patil, Sonali Patil, Shradha Vinayak Shilwant, Mily Lal, Manisha Bhende, Vijay Patil
publish: 2026 Innovations in Machine, Engineering, and Digital Conference (IMED)
abstract: Underwater wireless sensor networks play a critical role in long-term marine ecosystem monitoring; however, their performance is severely constrained by high energy consumption, dynamic network topology, and unreliable acoustic communication. This paper proposes the Dolphin Echolocation-Inspired Energy-Efficient Routing protocol, called DEI-EER, to improve data delivery in the underwater environment by utilizing the acoustic sensing and cooperative behaviours of dolphins. This approach leverages echolocation-based neighbor discovery to estimate link quality and range and uses a fitness-driven next-hop selection that jointly considers residual energy, acoustic reliability, and proximity to surface sinks. This increased robustness can be attributed to cooperative routing and intelligent route management that can better cope with the dynamic underwater environment. The simulations carried out show that the DEI-EER protocol achieves better results in reducing the amount of energy consumed as well as routing overhead, with higher PDRs and extended lifetime compared to the existing bio-inspired and learning-inspired routing protocols. This proves that biologically-inspired acoustic intelligence can act as a feasible and efficient option for developing efficient routing protocols for underwater sensor networks. In general, the proposed DEI-EER protocol can act as a reliable routing solution for underwater ecosystem observation as well as underwater sensing.

title: Evaluating Classical and Neural Network Models for Financial Bubble Detection: A Comparative Study of SVM and GRU
authors: Yuvaraj.S, Baskar Kasi, G. Sakthi, Jayanthi G
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Expert Clouds and Applications (ICOECA)
abstract: Financial bubbles pose significant threats to market stability, making their timely detection a crucial challenge in financial analytics. The objective of this study is to develop and evaluate a machine learning (ML)–based framework for stock market bubble detection by comparatively analyzing a traditional learning model (SVM) and a deep learning (DL) sequence model (GRU). The framework utilizes technical indicators such as the Relative Strength Index (RSI), Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD), Bollinger Bands, and a volume-based indicator, with model evaluation conducted on recent market data covering the testing period from 2022 to 2025. Experimental findings indicate that SVM achieves superior performance with a test accuracy of 98.59% and an AUC of 0.9952, outperforming GRU, which records a test accuracy of 98.12% and an AUC of 0.9880. Model performance was comprehensively assessed using classification reports, confusion matrices, Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves, and precision–recall analysis, confirming the robustness and effectiveness of the proposed framework. The findings highlight that kernel-based learning remains highly competitive in structured indicator-driven bubble detection scenarios.

title: Analog Implementation of a Fractional-Order PID Controller for the T200 Thruster Using a Novel Lattice Type Structure
authors: Yunus Emre Yokuş, Aslihan Kartci, Umut Engin Ayten, Roman Sotner, Jaroslav Koton
abstract: The T200 thruster is a widely used propulsion system for autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs); however, achieving high-performance control with low energy consumption remains a challenge due to inherent system nonlinearities and uncertainties. Fractional-order PID (FOPID) controllers offer enhanced flexibility and robustness compared to classical PID structures, making them well-suited for such complex dynamic systems; however, their practical analog realization and algorithm-based parameter optimization remain limited in the existing literature. This paper presents a unified control–hardware co-design framework for the T200 thruster by combining metaheuristic optimization, FOPID control theory, and low-power analog circuit design. Within this framework, a transfer function of the T200 thruster is obtained from experimental input–output data, and the parameters of the FOPID controller are optimized using multiple metaheuristic algorithms to enhance dynamic performance. A novel lattice-type circuit structure is then introduced for the analog implementation of the optimized FOPID controller. Comparative performance evaluation using transient response criteria and a series of statistical analysis methods demonstrates that the Salp Swarm Algorithm provides the most consistent and effective tuning results, yielding a well-damped closed-loop response with zero overshoot, improved transient behavior, and strong robustness against disturbances and parameter variations. SPICE simulations of the proposed lattice-type analog implementation show close agreement with MATLAB-based control results while achieving low power consumption of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$22.902~\mu $ </tex-math></inline-formula>W and reliable operation under process, voltage, temperature, and noise variations. These results confirm the feasibility and advantages of algorithm-optimized FOPID controllers for next-generation marine robotic systems in which high-precision control and low power consumption are crucial.

title: A U-Net based LSGAN Framework for 12-Lead ECG Synthesis in Wearable IoMT Systems
authors: Min Gu Kim, Jaehyo Jung
abstract: Wearable devices, such as smartwatches, offer a convenient way to monitor cardiac conditions through single- or few-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) recordings. However, these devices fail to provide the comprehensive diagnostic information available from the standard 12-lead ECG used in clinical settings. To address this technological gap, this paper proposes a novel framework for generating a complete 12-lead ECG from only two leads (Lead I and II). We designed a deep learning model based on a Least Squares Generative Adversarial Network (LSGAN) that employs a U-Net architecture as its generator. The U-Net effectively extracts multi-scale features and morphological details of the ECG signal, while the LSGAN framework ensures a stable training process and generates high-fidelity waveforms. Furthermore, an L1 loss was incorporated to enforce pixel-wise accuracy in the generated signals. Experimental results on the large-scale PTB-XL dataset, using a patient-wise split for evaluation, show that our proposed model achieves a high average Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC) of 0.905 across all ten generated leads, outperforming state-of-the-art methods. These findings confirm that our model can reliably generate clinically significant 12-lead ECGs, presenting a practical solution for enhancing remote cardiac monitoring in the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) environment.

title: Joint Sparsity and Beamforming Design for RDARS-Aided Systems
authors: Chengwang Ji, Haiquan Lu, Qiaoyan Peng, Jintao Wang, Shaodan Ma
abstract: Reconfigurable distributed antennas and reflecting surface (RDARS) has emerged as a promising architecture for communication performance enhancement. In particular, the new selection gain can be achieved by leveraging the dynamic working mode selection between connection and reflection modes, whereas low-complexity element configuration remains an open issue. In this paper, we consider a RDARS-assisted communication system, where the connected elements are formed as a uniform sparse array for simplified mode configuration. The sum rate maximization problem is then formulated by jointly optimizing the active and passive beamforming matrices and sparsity of connected element array. For the special cases of a single user equipment (UE) and two UEs, the optimal sparsity designs are derived in closed-form. Then, for an arbitrary number of UEs, a weighted minimum mean-square error-based alternating optimization (AO) algorithm is proposed to tackle the non-convex optimization problem. Numerical results demonstrate the effectiveness of low-complexity sparsity optimization.

title: RadioVAR: Fast Sampling-Free Radio Map Construction via Coarse-to-Fine Token Prediction
authors: Han Zhang, Yu Han, Hanhui Chen, Mengchen Yao, Yun Lin
abstract: Accurate spatial priors of received power spectral density (PSD) are important for communication-aware urban sensing and learning-based resource management. Sampling-free radio map (RM) construction avoids costly in-area measurements by directly constructing a full PSD-based radio map from environmental priors. However, mainstream diffusion-based methods incur high inference latency and lack an explicit coarse-to-fine generation path. To address these issues, we propose RadioVAR, a visual autoregressive (VAR) progressive framework for sampling-free RM construction. RadioVAR replaces iterative denoising with a feed-forward next-scale token prediction process, which substantially reduces inference computation and latency. RadioVAR first represents radio maps in a discrete multi-scale latent space using a frozen tokenizer, and performs coarse-to-fine next-scale token prediction conditioned on multi-channel priors (e.g., building layout, Tx location, and vehicle occupancy maps). We further introduce dynamic residual-token supervision to construct state-dependent token targets from the residual-to-go, improving training–inference alignment. Extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate that RadioVAR improves reconstruction quality over diffusion-based baselines while significantly reducing inference latency, thereby offering a better accuracy–efficiency trade-off for edge deployments.

title: Deep Information-Balanced Multimodal Learning_supp1-3681770.pdf
authors: Peng Hu
abstract: Multimodal learning aims to integrate diverse data sources to capture more comprehensive information about things, thus enhancing perception and understanding of the real world. However, inherent discrepancies between different modalities often lead to imbalanced optimization during multimodal learning, hindering performance improvement. To address this issue, in this paper, we present a Multimodal Information Balance (MIB) theory, grounded in Information Theory, to reveal that this imbalance arises from the imbalanced retention of complementary information during modality fusion, providing an intuitive and explainable perspective on the issue. Building on this insight, we propose a theoretical MIB criterion to adaptively balance the preservation of complementary information across individual modalities, thereby facilitating multimodal fusion. Using this criterion, we develop an Information-Balanced Multimodal Learning (IBML) framework to mine comprehensive and balanced multimodal information, achieving optimal learning. More specifically, IBML introduces Balance Information Optimization (BIO) module to maximize tractable lower bound objectives derived from the MIB criterion according to the optimization discrepancies across modalities, ensuring balanced retention of complementary information and enhancing information contributions during multimodal fusion. In addition, we present a supplementary and provable Task Complexity Modulation (TCM) module based on the MIB criterion to adjust task complexity discrepancies across input modalities, thus indirectly promoting the balanced preservation of complementary information throughout the learning process. Extensive experiments are conducted on eight multimodal datasets, spanning audio-visual recognition, image-text classification, and 2D-3D recognition, to verify the superiority and effectiveness of IBML. The code will be released publicly after in-peer review.

title: Multirate Finite-Control-Set Motor Parameter-Free Predictive Control for SPMSM Drives
authors: Xuesong Wu, Bowei Li, Cheng Xue, Yunwei Li
abstract: Compared to conventional single-rate schemes, the multirate finite-control-set model predictive control (FCS-MPC) allows higher switching frequencies at relatively low sampling rates. However, multirate schemes exhibit increased sensitivity to parameter variations, particularly as the number of subcycles increases. Although motor parameter-free prediction techniques can partially mitigate this sensitivity, their effectiveness within a multirate framework remains constrained by control parameters and nonlinear factors. To address these challenges, this work proposes an improved multirate FCS motor parameter-free predictive control (FCS-MPFPC) scheme. First, an adaptive control-parameter-tuning approach based on Kalman filters is introduced, which utilizes current variation information to recursively estimate the impact of input voltage. Second, corrected voltage vectors accounting for dead-time effects are incorporated into both the prediction process and observers. Furthermore, rotor movement effects are compensated using second-order generalized integrators (SOGI) and Lagrange interpolation. Experimental results validate the proposed method, demonstrating improved performance under both nominal and mismatched parameter conditions.

title: A Wearable Footstep Energy Harvester With Novel Dual-Clutch Mechanical Motion Rectification for Self-Powered Sensing_supp1-3675109.mp4
authors: Fang Deng
abstract: Wearable and autonomous sensing systems require efficient energy conversion under low-frequency, bidirectional mechanical excitations generated by daily human motion. This work presents a novel dual-clutch ratchet energy harvester (DREH) that converts bidirectional heel motions into unidirectional high-speed rotation, enabling direct DC power generation without conventional AC–DC rectification losses. The dual-clutch architecture ensures robust motion rectification under weak excitation and enables recovery of elastic potential energy during the liftoff phase, thereby improving effective energy utilization. A coupled dynamic–electromechanical model is developed to analyze the system behavior and predict electrical output. A compact prototype (86.4 cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>, 89 g) is fabricated and experimentally evaluated under pseudowalk and natural walking conditions. Experimental results show that elastic energy recovery increases output power by 59.5% at 0.3 Hz; at 6 km/h with a 75 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{\Omega}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> load, the prototype delivers 82 mW peak and 28 mW average power with a driving force below 14 N. System-level validation demonstrates continuous operation of a wireless sensing node, confirming the DREH as an effective self-sustained power source for wearable and industrial sensing applications.

title: Multiscale Graph Redefining: Correlation-Based Multiscale Graph Clustering Network for Human Motion Prediction_supp1-3684128.pdf
authors: Wenming Cao
abstract: Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) have exhibited considerable promise in 3-D skeleton-based human motion prediction. Based on the intuitive observation that human motion can be delineated through the physical interconnections among human joints, many previous works have designed multiscale graphs to learn the relationships and constraints between different graph scales, obtaining encouraging results for human motion prediction. However, these fixed multiscale graphs obtain new scale graphs by merging adjacent human joint information, ignoring implicit semantic information during dynamic movements. Furthermore, human joint correlations tend to vary randomly as the depth of the multiscale clustering graph increases, which contradicts the design concept of fixed multiscale graphs. To address these limitations, we explore a novel correlation-based multiscale graph clustering network (CMGC) for adaptive multiscale graph representation learning. Given a human joints graph, the goal of CMGC is first to generate more new graphs representing motion correlations adaptively at different scale levels and then selectively restore the derived graph scales to the original human joints graphs, which enables various motion features extraction. Moreover, we introduce the discrete wavelet transform (DWT) to compensate for the signal loss caused by discrete cosine transform (DCT) domain modeling from human motion. The CMGC gives rise to gratifying performances with the adaptive multiscale graph. Extensive experiments reveal that CMGC outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 11.2%, 10.1%, and 11.2% of 3-D mean per joint position error (MPJPE) on average on Human 3.6M, CMU Mocap, and 3DPW datasets, respectively. We also test the mean angle error (MAE) on Human3.6M, which is lower by 6.5% than previous methods. Our code is released at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/JunyuShi02/CMGC</uri>

title: Forgettable Federated Linear Learning With Certified Data Unlearning
authors: Ruinan Jin, Minghui Chen, Qiong Zhang, Xiaoxiao Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: The advent of federated learning (FL) has revolutionized the way distributed systems handle collaborative model training while preserving user privacy. Recently, federated unlearning (FU) has emerged to address demands for the “right to be forgotten” and unlearning of the impact of poisoned clients without requiring retraining in FL. Most FU algorithms require the cooperation of retained or target clients (clients to be unlearned), introducing additional communication overhead and potential security risks. In addition, some FU methods need to store historical models to execute the unlearning process. These challenges hinder the efficiency and memory constraints of the current FU methods. Moreover, due to the complexity of nonlinear models and their training strategies, most existing FU methods for deep neural networks (DNNs) lack theoretical certification. In this work, we introduce a novel FL training and unlearning strategy in DNN, termed forgettable federated linear learning (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> considers a common practice of using pretrained models to approximate DNN linearly, allowing them to achieve similar performance as the original networks via federated linear training (<monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FLT</monospace>). We then present <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FedRemoval</monospace>, a certified, efficient, and secure unlearning strategy that enables the server to unlearn a target client without requiring client communication or adding additional storage. We have conducted extensive empirical validation on small- to large-scale datasets, using both convolutional neural networks and modern foundation models (FMs). These experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> in balancing model accuracy with the successful unlearning of target clients. <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> represents a promising pipeline for efficient and trustworthy FU. The code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://anonymous.4open.science/r/2F2L-Federated-Unlearning-D57D/README.md</uri>

title: Scalable neural pushbroom architectures for real-time denoising of hyperspectral images onboard satellites
authors: Ziyao Yi, Davide Piccinini, Diego Valsesia, Tiziano Bianchi, Enrico Magli
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The next generation of Earth observation satellites will seek to deploy intelligent models directly onboard the payload in order to minimize the latency incurred by the transmission and processing chain of the ground segment, for time-critical applications. Designing neural architectures for onboard execution, particularly for satellite-based hyperspectral imagers, poses novel challenges due to the unique constraints of this environment and imaging system that are largely unexplored by the traditional computer vision literature. In this paper, we show that this setting requires addressing three competing objectives, namely high-quality inference with low complexity, dynamic power scalability and fault tolerance. We focus on the problem of hyperspectral image denoising, which is a critical task to enable effective downstream inference, and highlights the constraints of the onboard processing scenario. We propose a neural network design that addresses the three aforementioned objectives with several novel contributions. In particular, we propose a mixture of denoisers that can be resilient to radiation-induced faults as well as allowing for time-varying power scaling. Moreover, each denoiser employs an innovative architecture where an image is processed line-by-line in a causal way, with a memory of past lines, in order to match the acquisition process of pushbroom hyperspectral sensors and greatly limit memory requirements. We show that the proposed architecture can run in real-time, i.e., process one line in the time it takes to acquire the next one, on low-power hardware and provide competitive denoising quality with respect to significantly more complex state-of-the-art models. We also show that the power scalability and fault tolerance objectives provide a design space with multiple tradeoffs between those properties and denoising quality.

title: CLCT: Complementary Local Consensus Transformer for Two-View Correspondence Pruning
authors: Luanyuan Dai, Rui Yan, Jinhui Tang, Maocheng Zhao
abstract: Correspondence pruning extracts accurate correspondences (inliers) from initial ones, playing a crucial role in computer vision and robotics. This process is challenging due to the dominance of outliers and uneven correspondence distributions, which prevent fixed neighbor selection from reliably capturing local consistency and hinder the effective integration of local consistency into global modeling. To tackle these challenges, we introduce local consistency into the Transformer as a guidance signal for the first time, which allows it to adaptively perceive and enforce local matching constraints and consequently enhances global relationship modeling under uneven correspondence distributions. Motivated by this, we propose the Complementary Local Consensus Transformer (CLCT), which captures complementary local consistency by the Adaptive Feature Pruning (AFP) Graph and the Dynamic Weighted (DW) Graph and integrates them into Transformers to enhance global modeling. Specifically, the AFP graph adopts an adaptive pruning strategy to select high-quality correspondences with dynamic neighborhood adjustment, while the DW graph employs a Feature-Aware Distance block with Structural Salient Convolution to refine weighted neighborhood relationships. These graphs guide AFPTransformer and DWGTransformer, enabling a cooperative “point selection + edge weighting” mechanism that integrates local reliability with global reasoning. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed CLCT outperforms existing methods across multiple tasks.

title: CNIE: Content-Aware Non-Transferable Information Extraction for Fine-Grained Visual Categorization
authors: Qiaoqi Li, Shuo Ye, Yunfeng Diao, Dan Guo, Yantao Wei, Shutao Xia, Zitong Yu
abstract: Non-transferable learning (NTL) aims to restrict a model's generalization ability to a target domain. Existing methods primarily focus on coarse-grained tasks. However, the problem of NTL at the fine-grained level remains largely unexplored, mainly due to the difficulty in distinguishing subtle inter-class variations within the source domain while simultaneously suppressing transferable patterns that may generalize to the target domain. To this end, this paper proposes CNIE for this challenge, and the framework consists of three key components. The Fine-grained Prompt Representation (FPR) module effectively activates the rich semantic knowledge embedded in the pre-trained model by introducing mixed-granularity prompts and transfers it to downstream tasks. The Content-Aware Decoupling Model (CDM) disentangles visual features into two orthogonal components: content and context. The context captures latent domain-specific information to guide domain-level suppression and enhance non-transferability. The Non-Transferable Information Extraction (NIE) module encodes subclass-specific content, which is constrained by an information bottleneck to learn a minimal sufficient representation that preserves the intrinsic causal semantics of fine-grained categories. Both qualitative results and quantitative visualizations on three FGVC-related NTL datasets consistently demonstrate that the learned fine-grained feature representations achieve optimal non-transferability to the target domain with minimal degradation in the source domain, while also establishing new state-of-the-art results in non-transferable fine-grained representation learning.

title: Stochastic Forensics for Insider Threat Detection using Temporal and Behavioral Artifacts
authors: Mandapati Satya, Ponnaganti Nandan, R.Shalinirajan
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Expert Clouds and Applications (ICOECA)
abstract: The difficulty in separating legitimate and malicious practices of legitimate users has been a severe problem in the field of cybersecurity. Traditional log based forensic methods tend to be unsuccessful when the audit trails are incomplete, corrupted or deliberately altered. To overcome these shortcomings, this paper suggests a stochastic forensics model founded on the probabilistic modeling to match temporal and behavioural evidence of user activity. Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) are used to build baselines of behaviors and the Hidden Markov Models (HMM) are used to model sequential transitions between time in user behavior. Modeling of contextual and time-dependent anomalies are done using structured communication logs, especially email metadata, including timestamps, sender-recipient relations, attachment size, device identifiers and access frequency. Cross-validation is used to dynamically optimize probabilistic thresholds in order to balance the detection accuracy and false positives. This is shown by experimental analysis of real-world and synthetic data sets with a detection rate of 92, false positive rate of 5 and a mean response time of 2.5 seconds, which proves the usefulness of the proposed approach in real-time insider threat detection. In comparison with deterministic log-centric systems, the proposed approach is resilient to incomplete or spoilt log entries making it more forensic ready and situation aware in enterprise systems.

title: Automatic Cell-Aware Software-Based Self-Test Generation for a RISC-V Core Local Interrupt Controller
authors: Tobias Faller, Bernd Becker
abstract: Software-Based Self-Tests (SBST) allow for at-speed testing of on-chip devices via a processor core. However, creating SBSTs requires time-consuming manual labour that is expensive and requires in-depth knowledge of the device’s architecture for targeting hard-to-test faults. Introducing cell-aware testing to this task further exacerbates the necessary effort. In contrast, automating the complex parts of SBST generation using Bounded Model Checking (BMC) allows using sophisticated, state-of-theart BMC solvers to automatically generate vast parts of the SBST. Thereby, a virtual circuit called Validity Checker Module (VCM) is used to encode constraints for the SBST. In this paper, we focus on generating a cell-aware SBST for a Core Local Interrupt Controller (CLIC) that is executed via a RISC-V processor core. We first d erive a constraint s et that creates SBST-like conditions during ATPG, which allows for transformation into instructions later on. We implement a cellaware test model via a re-entrant Mealy state machine that allows complex behaviors of the faulty cell. Experimentally, we extend the CLIC with a test interface that is controlled via a custom CSR of the processor core to reduce SBST generation time and enhance fault coverage. We conclude with the evaluation of our approach on the PULP CLIC implementation that shows cellaware SBST generation to be feasible while achieving acceptable fault coverages and practical generation runtimes.

title: Microcontroller integrated sensor-based mobility aid for visually impaired people
authors: John Adebisi, Uunona Hilma Iyalooh N, Leokadia N. P. Ndjuluwa
publish: 2026 International Conference on ICT and Photonics (ICTP)
abstract: Technology is pervasively present in human life. The application of engineering in biomedical fields has significantly affected the people with special needs including the visually impaired. Visually impaired individuals face significant mobility challenges. Technologies, such as developing a mechanism to assist such individuals in navigating their environment safely and independently cannot be overemphasized. This work is not only designed to enhance existing effort in this area but also introduced an improved performance-based microcontroller integrated sensor that could assist visually impaired from different directions. The novelty of omnidirectional support of the designed stick makes it unique based on literature. This work demonstrates improved wayfinding, obstacle identification, and general movement, the stick incorporates sensors, and intuitive user interfaces. Furthermore, the output of this work placed high priority on the user comfort; it seeks to offer a more complete and user-friendly solution for navigating the environment. This design contributes to the creation of a more inclusive and supportive environment for visually impaired individuals.

title: Improving RO-PUFs on FPGAs: A Filtering Approach for Improved Reliability and Entropy
authors: Vasilii Kulagin, Giorgio Di Natale, Elena-Ioana Vatajelu
abstract: Physical Unclonable Functions (PUFs) have emerged in recent years as a reliable alternative to non-volatile memory for cryptographic applications. Their main advantage lies in the fact that they do not store data within the chip but generate unique responses on demand. However, some responses may be unreliable over time due to small variations in temperature or voltage, whether under normal operating conditions or during attacks, such as thermal attacks. This paper presents a novel filtering technique that has been applied to a ring oscillator PUF (RO-PUF) implemented on an FPGA, designed to address the issue of unreliable and biased responses.

title: How Long Can They Learn? A Methodology to Assess Endurance in Analog Memristive Synapses during Online Training
authors: Aleksandra Koroleva, Salah Daddinounou, Monica Burriel, Elena-Ioana Vatajelu
abstract: Memristive devices are widely explored as artificial synapses for hardware neuromorphic computing, offering analog programmability and in-memory learning capabilities. However, their limited endurance remains a critical concern for online training applications. In this work, we analyze the evolution of synaptic weights during spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP)based learning in a spiking neural network (SNN), to track weight updates and infer spike-timing dynamics during training. Our results show that synaptic activity is sparse and concentrated in a subset of connections, with many synapses remaining inactive throughout training. Moreover, even the most active synapses undergo relatively few significant updates, suggesting that the effective endurance stress is much lower than that assumed in conventional full-range cycling tests. We further propose that inferred timing differences can serve as a proxy for estimating precise electrical stress during training, enabling more accurate memristive endurance assessment during training. These findings support the feasibility of online learning with endurance-limited devices and offer insights for optimizing reliability in neuromorphic hardware.

title: Timely Trajectory Reconstruction in Finite Buffer Remote Tracking Systems
authors: Sunjung Kang, Vishrant Tripathi, Christopher G. Brinton
abstract: Remote tracking systems play a critical role in applications such as IoT, monitoring, surveillance and healthcare. In such systems, maintaining both real-time state awareness (for online decision making) and accurate reconstruction of historical trajectories (for offline post-processing) are essential. While the Age of Information (AoI) metric has been extensively studied as a measure of freshness, it does not capture the accuracy with which past trajectories can be reconstructed. In this work, we investigate reconstruction error as a complementary metric to AoI, addressing the trade-off between timely updates and historical accuracy. Specifically, we we consider a remote tracking system where the source evolves as a Wiener process and examine three policies, each prioritizing different aspects of information management: Keep-Old, Keep-Fresh, and our proposed Inter-arrival-Aware dropping policy. We compare these policies in terms of impact on both AoI and reconstruction error in a remote tracking system with a finite buffer. Through theoretical analysis and numerical simulations of queueing behavior, we demonstrate that while the Keep-Fresh policy minimizes AoI, it does not necessarily minimize reconstruction error. In contrast, our proposed Inter-arrival-Aware dropping policy dynamically adjusts packet retention decisions based on generation times, achieving a balance between AoI and reconstruction error. Our results provide key insights into the design of efficient buffer management policies for resource-constrained IoT networks.

title: IEEE Standard for Ethernet Amendment 11: Bidirectional 100 Gb/s Optical Access PHYs
authors: N/A
abstract: This amendment to IEEE Std 802.3-2022 adds Physical Layer specifications and management parameters for 100 Gb/s Ethernet optical interfaces for bidirectional operation over a single strand of single-mode fiber with reaches of at least 10 km, 20 km, and 40 km.

title: Dynamic Event-Triggered Formation Tracking Control of Unmanned Surface Vehicles with Prescribed Performance via Prescribed-Time Disturbance Observer
authors: Yakun Zhu, Zexi Li, Chao Yuan, Yaosen Li, Ge Guo
abstract: This paper studies the prescribed-time unmanned surface vehicles(USVs) formation control. First, to eliminate the effect of disturbances, a prescribed-time disturbance observer( PTDO) is designed without requiring prior knowledge of the disturbance boundary, which can accurately observe the disturbances within a prescribed-time. Second, to ensure the formation tracking performance under abrupt disturbances, a novel multi-threshold prescribed performance function(PPF) is designed, whose parameters can be flexibly adjusted to cope with abrupt disturbances. Third, to reduce unnecessary controller updates, a novel dynamic event-triggered mechanism (DETM) is proposed, whose triggered parameter is prescribed-time stable, thereby yielding longer triggering intervals and reducing resource consumption. Fourth, based on the above designs, a prescribedtime formation tracking controller is developed to ensure that the USV system achieves the desired formation within the prescribed time. Finally, simulation results verify the correctness and superiority of the method.

title: Fish-Scale LA-GNB Deployment for Low-Altitude Networks: Coverage-Interference Tradeoffs in Dense Airspaces
authors: Mengdi Li, Fen Hou, Hongwei Ding, Bo Li
abstract: With the rapid growth of low-altitude airspace utilization and the increasing communication demands of Aerial User Equipment (UE) below 300 m dense urban areas urgently require efficient regional coverage solutions. However, existing approaches, primarily focused on ground-level or high-altitude scenarios, struggle with challenges specific to low altitudes, including antenna null zones, complex cell relationships, and interference. These solutions often fail to balance link reliability, interference suppression, and deployment costs effectively. To address these challenges, we propose integrating inter-frequency active antenna units (AAUs) as low-altitude gNBs (LA-gNBs) within urban macro (UMa) environments, forming a dedicated inter-frequency network. By employing a novel Fish-Scale deployment strategy—a geometrically overlapping cell layout that resembles fish scales to ensure uniform and low-interference coverage—we establish the Fish-Scale LA-gNB planning scheme designed for cost-effective and highly reliable low-altitude coverage. We developed a customized Clustered Delay Line (CDL) channel model using ray-tracing-based configuration to accurately simulate complex low-altitude propagation environments. Performance was rigorously evaluated through joint system-level and link-level simulations. Experimental results demonstrate that the Fish-Scale LA-gNB planning scheme can achieve a superior balance between Reference Signal Received Power (RSRP) and Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR) through a uniform, low-interference base station layout. Compared to conventional schemes, this approach provides excellent coverage and nearly eliminates link failures and handover failures, proving its robustness in dynamic low-altitude scenarios even at high speeds. Critically, this performance is achieved using only one-third of the AAUs required by traditional gNB setups, highlighting its significant cost-effectiveness. This study offers both theoretical insights and a practical, high-performance solution for low-altitude wireless network planning.

title: Design of Polar Subcodes for Permutation Decoding
authors: Mikhail Chernikov, Peter Trifonov
abstract: novel construction of randomized polar subcodes and subcodes of extended BCH codes is presented. The proposed approach stems from a new heuristic for building polar code frozen set which minimizes the error probability of successive cancellation algorithm applied to permuted noisy codewords. The frame error rate (FER) of the proposed codes under permutationbased decoding is comparable to FER of existing polar subcodes under successive cancellation list or sequential algorithm.

title: DIJIT: A Robotic Head for an Active Observer_supp1-3682980.mp4
authors: Mostafa Kamali Tabrizi

title: Natural Language Interfaces for Spatial and Temporal Databases: A Comprehensive Overview of Methods, Taxonomy, and Future Directions
authors: Samya Acharja, Kanchan Chowdhury
abstract: The task of building a natural language interface to a database, known as NLIDB, has recently gained significant attention from both the database and Natural Language Processing (NLP) communities. There have been many research works that study the problem of automatically converting natural language questions into database queries. With the proliferation of geospatial datasets driven by the rapid emergence of location-aware sensors, geospatial databases play a vital role in supporting geospatial applications. However, querying geospatial and temporal databases differs substantially from querying traditional relational databases due to the presence of geospatial topological operators and temporal operators. To bridge the gap between geospatial query languages and non-expert users, the geospatial research community has increasingly focused on developing NLIDBs for geospatial databases. Yet, existing research remains fragmented across systems, datasets, and methodological choices, making it difficult to clearly understand the landscape of existing methods, their strengths and weaknesses, available benchmarks, and opportunities for future research. Existing surveys on NLIDBs focus on general-purpose database systems and do not treat geospatial and temporal databases as primary focus for analysis. To address this gap, this paper presents a comprehensive survey of studies on NLIDBs for geospatial and temporal databases. Specifically, we first provide a detailed overview of datasets and evaluation metrics used to train and evaluate geospatial and temporal NLIDBs. We then study the taxonomy of the methods for geospatial and temporal NLIDBs and provide a comprehensive summary and comparative analysis of the existing methods. Our survey reveals recurring trends in existing methods, substantial variation in datasets and evaluation practices, and several open challenges that continue to hinder progress in this area. Based on these findings, we identify promising directions for future research to advance natural language interfaces to geospatial and temporal databases.

title: Target Speaker Isolation for Mobile Health Assessment from Speech with Crosstalk
authors: Apiwat Ditthapron, Adam C. Lammert, Emmanuel O. Agu
abstract: Speech assessments are crucial for monitoring medical conditions such as Parkinson’s disease, depression, and Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI). However, crosstalk—speech from non-target speakers—presents a critical challenge in real-world mobile health assessments, often leading to false diagnoses. Continuous, real-world speech assessments using mobile devices are realistic but require additional pre-processing steps to ensure speech quality. Isolating the target speaker’s speech is necessary before health assessments. Previous research has focused on removing background noise but paid limited attention to crosstalk in passive health assessments. Some prior work addressed crosstalk using speech separation, which is found to create artifacts on the extracted speech. Instead, we propose Target Speaker Isolation with Normal Distribution (TSI-N) to isolate speech samples of the target speaker. Unlike standard embeddings, TSI-N utilizes an N-vector speaker representation trained to fit an unbounded normal distribution for each speaker cluster. This probabilistic property enables robust null-hypothesis testing and rapid online learning on resource-constrained mobile devices. In rigorous evaluations on various levels of crosstalk, TSI-N significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving accuracy improvements of up to 36% for TBI detection and 18% for depression detection in passive speech assessments.

title: Community-Aware Information Gating for Robust Bot Detection
authors: Jun Li, Wentao Jiang, Tingwen Liu, Peng Zhang, Liyan Shen
abstract: The convergence of social networks and the Internet of Things (IoT) forms a complex digital ecosystem that links physical sensor data with human social interactions. However, the growing presence of social bots and fake sensor accounts introduces deceptive behaviors that distort relationships and undermine trust in IoT-enabled social platforms. Existing graph neural network (GNN) approaches have shown promise in social bot detection, yet most are built on the homophily assumption, which constrains their capacity to capture adversarial relationships between genuine and malicious entities. To address this challenge, we propose CAGNet, a Community-Aware Gated Network that jointly models temporal and structural properties. Specifically, user posts are represented as temporal sequences through a recurrent neural network to capture dynamic behavioral patterns, while local clustering coefficients and neighbor feature deviations guide an adaptive fusion of neighbor information and node residuals. Empirical results demonstrate that CAGNet effectively captures both temporal and structural dependencies, enhancing the model’s resilience against adversarial manipulation and improving the reliability of social bot detection in IoT-driven environments. Furthermore, we introduce the expert-annotated Chinese Weibo benchmark for social bot detection, offering a high-quality, reusable resource for evaluation. Experimental results show that the proposed method improves accuracy by 2.01% over current methods.

title: Non-Intrusive Contactless Gesture Recognition for Human-Robot Interaction
authors: Dimitrios Tsiakmakis, Massimiliano Iosi, Gastone Ciuti
abstract: As robots increasingly integrate into collaborative and assistive environments, natural gesture interpretation becomes crucial for effective human-robot interaction. While vision-based gesture recognition systems face privacy concerns and performance limitations under occlusion and varying lighting conditions, wearable sensor approaches often compromise user comfort and interaction naturalness. These challenges necessitate alternative approaches that balance recognition accuracy with user acceptance in real-world deployment scenarios. This work proposes a lightweight transformer-based feature extractor where test-time augmentation with majority voting is employed during inference to enhance classification performance. With approximately 2M trainable parameters, the network maintains reasonable efficiency on modern acceleration hardware. Its modular architecture also indicates an easy adaptation to a variety of sensor configurations. It reached an overall accuracy of 97.21%, with similarly high scores for f1-score, precision, and recall. The pipeline's inference duration of 16 ms on GPU and 40 ms on CPU demonstrate its suitability for real-time applications in collaborative robotics.

title: LoD-GS: Robust and Lightweight Gaussian Splatting SLAM for Real-Time Volumetric Scene Reconstruction
authors: Jiachen Wang, Seung-Hyun Kong
abstract: Real-time 3D reconstruction is becoming a key enabler for robotics, mixed reality, and autonomous vehicles. Recent advances in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have enabled high-fidelity volumetric modeling, and their integration with SLAM shows strong potential for real-time deployment. However, the substantial size of 3DGS models limits deployment on heterogeneous devices, while their rendering quality remains highly sensitive to tracking accuracy under motion blur and abrupt texture variations. In this work, we propose LoD-GS, a lightweight and robust 3DGS-SLAM framework that produces compact yet high-fidelity Gaussian scene representations for flexible deployment. LoD-GS integrates entropy-driven scene-complete volumetric mapping to improve pose quality and Gaussian initialization, a geometry-aware rendering quality optimizer that emphasizes near-field and structure-rich regions under limited optimization budgets, and a deployment-aware level-of-detail 3DGS compression module that enables adaptive resource-quality tradeoffs. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks and real-world office sequences demonstrate its effectiveness, reducing model size by up to 53.8%, increasing rendering FPS by up to 43.72%, and improving PSNR by up to 2.471 dB.

title: Counterfactual Risk Minimization for Out-of-Distribution Generalization
authors: Yanhua Yang, Muli Yang, Jiahua Li, Henan Wang, Cheng Deng, Hongyuan Zhu
abstract: The out-of-distribution (OOD) property in data is deemed as one main challenge hindering the generalization ability of machine learning algorithms. However, the underlying reasons for this property remain an intriguing and open question that has yet to be fully understood. In this paper, we seek to enhance our understanding of the OOD phenomenon by framing it as a problem of distribution shift and addressing it through two complementary causal perspectives. The first is a generative causal view that elucidates the data generation process. We introduce a novel three-dimensional coordinate system to represent three fundamental distribution shifts, illustrating their role in various OOD generalization problems. The second is an anti-causal view that focuses on the model learning process. We develop an effective approach dubbed Counterfactual Risk Minimization (CRM) to address arbitrary distribution shifts in a unified framework. Additionally, we introduce a new multidomain visual recognition dataset called CONA to facilitate further exploration of OOD generalization. We conduct evaluations of CRM alongside several state-of-the-art competitors on four benchmark datasets under the three distribution shifts. The results not only affirm CRM’s superiority but also shed light on potential future directions.

title: LB-PTQ: Effective Low-Bit Post-Training Quantization for Vision Transformers
authors: Zhe Wang, Kaixin Xu, Xue Geng, Jie Lin, Mohamed M. Sabry Aly, Min Wu, Xiaoli Li, Weisi Lin
abstract: Recently, Vision Transformers (ViTs) have become the state-of-the-art architecture on various computer vision tasks including image classification, object detection and semantic segmentation. However, such success in high-accuracy performance comes at the price of high computational complexity, with typically tens of millions of or even more parameters in a Vision Transformer (ViT) model. Such a large volume of parameters makes it very difficult to deploy ViT models on mobile devices and cumbers their applications. In this paper, we present a novel post-training quantization approach that is able to quantize ViT models to very low bit widths, without the need of re-training. Prior works on post-training quantization for ViTs optimize the quantization of each layer separately thus leading to sub-optimal results. In contrast, we propose a unified learning framework that jointly optimizes the quantization of all layers to directly reduce the overall output error of the network. Moreover, we explore an important property of ViTs, i.e., the additivity property, revealing that the output error caused by the quantization of multiple layers equals the sum of the output error due to the quantization of each layer. Utilizing this property, we present a very efficient algorithm to solve the joint optimization problem with linear time complexity. We performed extensive experiments on the large-scale ImageNet dataset to evaluate the effectiveness of our approach. Empirical results show that our approach improves state-of-the-art noticeably on various ViT models and lowers the bit width from 8-bit to 6-bit without hurting the accuracy. Specifically, at 4 bits, our approach significantly outperforms existing works by 1.72%, 11.49%, 6.15%, and 3.54% on ViTS, ViT-B, DeiT-S, and DeiT-B, respectively. In the end, we evaluate the performance when deploying our quantized models on hardware. Our approach achieves 1.5ˆ to 1.7ˆ speedups for the inference on NVIDIA A100 GPU.

title: Self-Organized Team Formation via Multi-Task Hedonic Games for Capability-Heterogeneous Human-Machine Agents
authors: Tian-Yu Zuo, Pan Li, Kai Di, Yichuan Jiang, Yuangan Wang, Boon Han Lim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Partitioning a pool of capability-heterogeneous human and machine agents into effective teams for multiple concurrent tasks is a fundamental challenge in hybrid human–machine collaboration. We formalize this problem as a Multi-Task Additively Separable Hedonic Game (MT-ASHG), in which every agent is a self-interested player whose utility combines (i) a task-specific proficiency score measuring how well the agent’s skill vector aligns with task requirements, and (ii) an inter-agent compatibility score capturing synergistic or conflicting partnerships. Building on this formulation, we design an Iterative Best Response (IBR) algorithm that lets agents autonomously migrate between task groups to improve their individual payoffs. We prove that the IBR dynamics converge in a finite number of steps to an individually stable partition, in which no agent can unilaterally improve its utility by switching teams, and analyze the computational complexity of the convergence process. To bridge theory and practice, we further introduce an empirical profiling method that extracts capability and compatibility vectors from historical agent interactions, addressing the cold-start problem in newly formed human–machine teams. Extensive experiments on synthetic benchmarks and the Overcooked-AI cooperative environment demonstrate that MT-ASHG consistently outperforms centralized assignment baselines and existing coalition-formation methods in terms of global task completion rate, fairness, and scalability.

title: A Global-Local Data Clustering Framework With Adaptive Feature and Sample Weighting and Balanced Constraint
authors: Yong Peng, Mimi Jin, Yiyan Wang, Yuxin Chen, Feiwei Qin, Wanzeng Kong, Andrzej Cichocki
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Clustering remains a hot research topic in machine learning and data mining communities. The underlying data similarity serves as the foundation of clustering data into respective groups. However, since different features have different discriminative capabilities and different samples might correspond to different importance levels, it is inaccurate to capture the data similarity if we ignore any of them in real applications. By taking both into consideration, we propose an adaptive feature and sample weighting (AFSW) scheme to quantitatively measure the importance in the least square regression sense, serving as a global clustering. As a local complementary, a graph is utilized to facilitate cluster indicator matrix learning, leading to a global-local (GL) clustering framework. As a secondary contribution, an entropy-based balanced constraint is introduced to enforce the cluster sizes to be close, which is a reasonable assumption if we are clueless to a certain given data set. In concrete model formulation, two strategies respectively based on self-paced learning and exponential probability coefficient are designed to determine the sample importance. The formulated model, termed GLAFSW, is optimized efficiently within an alternating direction framework. Experiments are performed on both synthetic and benchmark data sets from diverse aspects. By comparing the proposed framework against the state-of-the-arts, the results verified that it achieved competitive performance in clustering.

title: Design and Experimental Validation of a 25-kW Dual DAB–CLLC Bidirectional DC/DC Converter With an Ultrawide 200–1000 V Operating Range
authors: Jeong-Won Yeom, Chang-Min Lim, Seung-Min Jeong, Il-Oun Lee
abstract: This article presents the design and experimental validation of a 25-kW bidirectional DC/DC converter employing a dual DAB-CLLC resonant architecture to achieve high efficiency across an ultrawide 200–1000-V operating range. The proposed topology connects two DAB–CLLC submodules in parallel on the input side and reconfigures them in series or parallel on the battery side according to the voltage level. This reconfigurable dual-module structure enables high power density, balanced current sharing, and minimized conduction losses under low-voltage conditions, while reducing switching stress during high-voltage operation. Furthermore, a dynamic DC-link voltage control and an integrated control algorithm are developed to coordinate bidirectional power flow and maintain stable transition between charging and discharging modes. Analytical derivation of the resonant-tank parameters and gain characteristics is provided, followed by an optimal design guideline ensuring Zero-Voltage Switching (ZVS) under both directions of power flow. A 25-kW prototype implemented with 1200-V-class SiC devices achieves 98.37-% peak efficiency in charging and 97.82-% in discharging, verified experimentally over the full 200–1000-V range. Compared with previously reported DAB-CLLC and reconfigurable resonant converters, the proposed system demonstrates wider voltage adaptability, simpler hardware implementation, and superior thermal reliability, proving its suitability for next-generation bidirectional fast-charging systems for electric vehicles.

title: Overcoming Data Scarcity in Retinal Lesion Segmentation via Instance Injection and Multi-Scale Ensembling
authors: Venkatesh Bhat, P.C. Siddalingaswamy, Manamohana K
publish: 2026 Twelfth International Conference on Bio Signals, Images, and Instrumentation (ICBSII)
abstract: Automated segmentation of retinal lesions is a prerequisite for the early diagnosis of Diabetic Retinopathy (DR). However, the development of robust models is hindered by the extreme scarcity of pixel-wise annotations and the severe class imbalance between structural features and minute pathologies. In this work, we propose a data-efficient framework that improves upon the standard Swin-UNet architecture. We introduce a Coordinate-Attention Fusion module to recover spatial information lost during downsampling and implement a novel training pipeline integrating Copy-Paste Instance Injection and Multi-Scale Zoom Augmentation. To ensure statistical robustness, we employ a 5-Fold Cross-Validation Ensemble. Benchmarking against a standard ResNet34 U-Net baseline on the IDRiD dataset, our method demonstrates superior convergence. While the baseline suffers from oversegmentation (Mean IoU: 0.2346), our proposed framework achieves a Mean IoU of 0.3606, representing a 53.7% relative improvement. Notably, we achieve state-of-the-art recovery of challenging Soft Exudates (IoU 0.58 vs. Baseline 0.12).

title: Federated Machine Learning Approach for Multi-Protocol IoT Intrusion Detection for Healthcare
authors: Ashwin S, Shreenithi M, Roshni Rangarajan, Ganesan M
publish: 2026 Twelfth International Conference on Bio Signals, Images, and Instrumentation (ICBSII)
abstract: This paper aims to present an Adaptive Federated Multi-Protocol Intrusion Detection System (AFM-IDS) to protect Internet of Things (IoT) environments which is used in many smart healthcare systems through a privacy-preserving and decentralised learning approach. Unlike conventional intrusion detection systems, instead of depending on centralised data and focusing on a single protocol, AFM-IDS uses Federated Learning (FL). This enables collaborative model development across heterogeneous IoT nodes or clients. A publicly available dataset, “ACI-IoT-2023”, was preprocessed and distributed among multiple virtual devices for federated training. Each device trained a local neural network on protocol-specific traffic and sent encrypted model parameters to a central server for aggregation. The global model was evaluated with accuracy, precision, recall and F1-score metrics. AFM-IDS provides low latency, scalability and robust detection. Hence, this is a practical and adaptable framework for healthcare systems, which nowadays are very crucial to be handled with proper and safe usage.

title: Optimal Sensor Placement for Fall Detection: A Dual-Sensor Framework with Multi-Mode Motion Analysis
authors: Poojasaai Gokulnath, G. A. Jayaashri, M. Mohamed Fargik, S. Poonguzhali
publish: 2026 Twelfth International Conference on Bio Signals, Images, and Instrumentation (ICBSII)
abstract: Reliable fall detection remains a fundamentally unsolved challenge in wearable safety systems due to the complex biomechanics of human impact events and the difficulty of separating falls from high-variance normal walking patterns. This work introduces and experimentally validates a fall-detection algorithm that was developed, which leverages dynamic acceleration-magnitude profiling and adaptive thresholding for real-time impact characterization. To rigorously assess the algorithm's biomechanical sensitivity, we employed high-precision Xsens MTw Awinda IMUs as a reference instrumentation system. Quantitative evaluation using various features demonstrates a clear dominance of central torso placements, with hip and navel yielding the strongest fall signatures and earliest impact localization. The results provide high-fidelity experimental validation of the proposed algorithm and decisively establish torso-mounted sensing as the optimal configuration for robust, real-time fall-detection architectures.

title: Confidence-Aware and Explainable Deep Learning for Ovarian Cancer Subtype and Risk Mapping
authors: Harshini J, Fathimathu Liya PP, Hepsiba D
publish: 2026 Twelfth International Conference on Bio Signals, Images, and Instrumentation (ICBSII)
abstract: Ovarian cancer is one of the most aggressive gynecological malignancies and this is mainly attributed to late diagnosis, recurrence and strong histopathological heterogeneity. Accurate detection of ovarian cancer subtypes important in determining the treatment plans and enhancing patient survival. Traditional diagnostic procedures rely on manual histopathological examination, which is subjective, time-consuming and prone to inter-observer variability, inspiring the process of adopting automated and effective computer-aided diagnostic systems. In this paper, the authors suggest a Confidence-Aware and Explainable Deep Learning model that can be used to classify ovarian cancer subtypes and map risks based on histopathological images automatically. A tuned EfficientNet-B0 model is used and compared with MobileNetV2, a lightweight baseline model. The framework combines the entropy-based confidence estimation and explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) through Grad-CAM to increase the reliability and interpretability of predictions. The experimental findings indicate that the proposed model has a validation accuracy of 91.01% with test-time augmentation (TTA), outperforming the baseline while providing higher transparency and clinical applicability of computational pathology.

title: Investigation of EEGNet and its variants for ADHD Detection: Joint Spatial, Temporal, and Frequency Domain Learning
authors: Kuricheti Prerana, Niharika Sharma, Diya Dinesh, Pavani Satwika, Amrutha Veluppal
publish: 2026 Twelfth International Conference on Bio Signals, Images, and Instrumentation (ICBSII)
abstract: Early and reliable detection of Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) through EEG analysis holds much promise for developing objective and accessible neurodiagnostics. However, the conventional EEG- based ADHD detection approaches usually process the spatial, temporal, and frequency information separately, which leads to fragmented representations and limit the model's ability to learn representations from EEG data. In the study, we propose an end to end ADHD detection framework that uses EEGNet and modified EEGNet which are both compact convolutional networks used to classify different EEG-based brain computer interface applications. The models learns frequency, temporal and spatial domain representations together which enables the model to extract EEG patterns which reflect ADHD related neural activity. To avoid data leakage and evaluate the model's performance, the dataset was evaluated using subject-wise splitting through Group KFold cross validation. The model achieved an accuracy of 97 % when it was evaluated using sample-wise splitting. However, this setup introduces data leakage due to overlap of subject specific EEG characteristics across training and testing sets. These results highlight EEGNet as an efficient approach for ADHD detection from EEG signals.

title: Design and Development of a Smart Augmented Reality Vision Glass Usage in Healthcare Monitoring, Enhanced Learning and Navigation Purpose
authors: Maheswari E, Saisubramani H S, Sarvesvar S, Sabarish K S
publish: 2026 Twelfth International Conference on Bio Signals, Images, and Instrumentation (ICBSII)
abstract: The Smart AR Vision Glass is a low-cost and portable wearable Augmented Reality (AR) device based on NodeMCU (ESP8266) technology, which can be applied in health care, primary education, and navigation support. The proposed Smart AR Vision Glass design integrates biomedical sensors for the measurement of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SpO}_{2}$</tex>, blood glucose level, body temperature, and blood pressure, with vital signs information presented using an OLED-based head-up display for continuous and unobtrusive hospital monitoring. In an educational environment, the proposed Smart AR Vision Glass design enhances learning by presenting interactive visual information to primary school children. For delivery and mobility service providers, the proposed Smart AR Vision Glass design offers real-time navigation support using wireless connectivity and smartphone integration. The proposed design emphasizes energy efficiency, portability, and seamless IoT communication. Experimental results confirm the accuracy of sensor data, robust wireless communication, and clear visual information display. The Smart AR Vision Glass is a scalable and affordable wearable AR platform for diverse real-world applications.

title: Distributed Random Space-Time Coding for Unmanned Ground Vehicles in Barrage Relay Networks
authors: Meng Wang, Fengye Hu, Zhuang Ling, Yayun Tan
abstract: We propose a distributed random space-time coding based on rotated Alamouti code to address the loss of orthogonality in Alamouti codes and the resulting inter-symbol interference (ISI) caused by link asymmetry in distributed cooperation of high-density unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs) barrage relay networks (BRNs) under complex tactical environments. This approach incorporates rotation coding techniques, applying specific angular rotations to the coding matrix to circumvent ISI without increasing relay communication overhead, thereby restoring system diversity gains. Combined with random space-time coding (RSTC), it utilizes a randomly weighted coefficient matrix to enhance spatial diversity capabilities and physical layer security in multipath fading channels. Furthermore, a composite forest channel model incorporating path loss, vegetation attenuation, and shadow fading is constructed, with end-to-end outage probability derived. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme achieves signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) gain over the conventional phase rotation (PR)-based BRN, while significantly reducing outage probability. This validates its effectiveness and reliability in tactical environments.

title: Optimizing Incoming Quality Control with a Dynamic Set Covering Strategy for Zero-Defect Inspection
authors: Wenqing Zhou, Yufeng Chen, Rui Zhou, Mohamed Sharaf
abstract: This paper develops an adaptive incoming quality control framework that integrates a dynamic set-covering strategy to support zero-defect inspection in large-scale production, ensuring complete identification of non-conforming parts while reducing inspection time and cost. Conventional set-covering approaches typically guarantee full coverage at limited batch sizes, and often fail to maintain both detection completeness and operational efficiency in large-scale production. To address this limitation, we reformulate the inspection process using a two-phase dynamic set-covering mechanism. In the first phase, a baseline set is established by hierarchically prioritizing inspection items. Items detecting more non-conforming parts are assigned a higher priority. The remaining items that do not contribute to current coverage are subsequently ranked according to their inherent defect probabilities. In the second phase, a reduced inspection item subset is selected from the prioritized set to cover all observed non-conforming parts in the current batch. The proposed strategy operates through two interconnected mechanisms to ensure practical defect coverage. It establishes explicit coverage constraints and employs an adaptive update rule that refines item priorities across batches based on residual inspection information. Finally, we present an example to demonstrate the proposed approach.

title: Development of a Bernoulli Suction-Type Non-Contact Manipulator Capable of Controlling Object Position and Orientation During Holding_supp1-3685899.mp4
authors: Takeshi Takaki
abstract: In this study, we propose a Bernoulli suction-type non-contact manipulator that can attract an object and simultaneously change its in-plane position and orientation while holding it. The proposed manipulator consists of a suction plate equipped with six compressed-air nozzles.
