title: Privacy-Preserving Cross-Chain Federated Blockchain for Carbon-Credit Management
authors: C.Madhusudhana Rao, Praveen Kumar Naidu Rayanki, Polepalli Rajeev Meenon, Salapakshi Jai Kumar, Ganga Kaveri Nimmakayala
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Expert Clouds and Applications (ICOECA)
abstract: The rapid growth of the carbon-credit markets in China has revealed serious flaws in the transparency, security and interoperability across sectors, especially in the automotive PCFN regulatory, ecosystem. The currently deployed blockchain-IoT systems enhance traceability but do not have privacy-protective insight, scalable verification, and interledger dialogue needed to facilitate multi-sector carbon governance. The paper will offer a Privacy-Preserving Cross-Chain Federated Blockchain-IoT Framework, which will combine RFID-enabled real-time capture of emissions, cross-chain interoperability via IBC (Inter-Blockchain Communication) and a differential-privacy-enhanced federated learning (DP-FL) engine to detect fraud and make dynamic predictions of carbon-credit prices. The system uses the hybrid DPoS-sidechain architecture to enable it to achieve high-throughput validation and ensure high-level data sovereignty assurances across the heterogeneous enterprises. The experimental analysis based on simulated national automotive and energy-sector datasets indicates that the suggested framework can help lower inter-chain verification latency by 74.8 percent, increase the cross-sector audit transparency by 41.3 percent, and increase the rate of fraud/anomaly detection by 93.5 percent to 96.8 percent in the case of integrating DP-FL. Moreover, advanced transactions through smart contracts are cross-chain automated settlement which results into overhead of transaction decreased by 52.4 percent and market liquidity enhanced by 35.6 percent in multi-sector simulations. The findings affirm that the suggested system would bolster security, scalability, and intelligence in the carbon-credit ecosystem of China to a considerable extent, providing a scalable base to support countrywide carbon-neutrality goals.

title: Optimization of 3D Reconstruction Algorithm for Virtual Reality Environment Based on Computer Vision
authors: Shensen Li
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Communication Networks and Computing (CNC)
abstract: When applying 3D reconstruction technology in virtual reality field, it is find that the real-time performance of 3D reconstruction algorithm is poor, and the details reconstruction is not very accurate in the dynamic scenes. In this paper, a hybrid 3D reconstruction algorithm is proposed based on computer vision. Firstly, the multi-scale feature fusion mechanism is proposed. Secondly, an iterative optimization strategy is designed to use the non-rigid deformation model to handle the motion estimation of dynamic objects and improve the temporal continuity. Finally, a parallel computing framework based on GPU (Graphics Processing Unit) is constructed. Finally, a semantic segmentation module is introduced to optimize the topological structure of the reconstruction results based on context. The experimental results show that the average Chamfer Distance of the static scene is decreased to 0.696mm, the reconstruction error of the dynamic scene is controlled within 1.52mm, and the frame rate of the processing is 26.4 FPS. The conclusion of this research is that the proposed algorithm improves the performance of 3D reconstruction in virtual reality environment through multi-scale feature fusion and dynamic optimization.

title: Smart Video Surveillance System with Intruder Identification and Behavioural Anomaly Detection using YOLOv8
authors: R. Anto Pravin, S. Akalya, V. Harinipriya
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Expert Clouds and Applications (ICOECA)
abstract: The smart video surveillance system with intruder identification and behavioural anomaly detection using YOLOv8 aims to enhance security monitoring through intelligent automation. Traditional surveillance systems require manual observation, making them inefficient and prone to human error. Existing methods often fail to accurately detect intruders or analyse unusual behaviour in real time. To overcome these limitations, this project integrates YOLOv8, a state-of-the-art deep learning object detection model, for precise and rapid identification of people and intruders. The system will also include behavioural anomaly detection using motion tracking and pattern analysis to recognize suspicious activities. Implementation involves real-time video streaming, object tracking and alert generation using Python and OpenCV. The model will be trained on security datasets to improve detection accuracy. The expected outcome is an automated, high-accuracy surveillance system that minimizes false alarms and enhances situational awareness in security-sensitive environments.

title: Enhancing U-Net with Attention-Guided Decoders and Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling for Tomato Plant Disease Segmentation
authors: E. Jansi, Kavitha BR
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Communication Networks and Computing (CNC)
abstract: In this paper, we propose AGAD-U-Net, a novel U-Net variant designed for precise plant leaf disease segmentation through an Attention-Guided Aggregation Decoder (AGAD). While traditional U-Net architectures rely on direct skip connections and standard upsampling operations, the AGAD module enhances the decoder by selectively integrating multi-scale attention-refined features, aiming to improve spatial consistency and feature fusion. To evaluate the effectiveness of AGAD-U-Net, we conducted a comparative analysis with baseline U-Net, Dilated U-Net, and ASPP-U-Net on a plant disease segmentation dataset. Experimental results show that while Dilated U-Net achieved the highest overall performance, AGAD-U-Net demonstrated competitive precision (0.6758) and accuracy (0.9431), and outperformed the baseline U-Net across multiple metrics. Although its IoU and F1 score were comparatively lower, AGAD-U-Net exhibited strong boundary refinement in qualitative observations. The findings suggest that the AGAD module offers a promising foundation for future decoder-level attention frameworks and motivates further optimization to fully exploit its capabilities in real-world agricultural applications.

title: Multi-Modal Fusion Approach Combining LSTM and CNN Models for Parkinson’s Detection from Speech and Handwriting Data
authors: Guntumadugu Venkata Sravan, Guntumadugu Revanth, Gangireddy Vishnu Vardhan Reddy, Ajay Sharma, Kamalpreet Kaur, Rachit Garg
publish: 2026 Innovations in Machine, Engineering, and Digital Conference (IMED)
abstract: Parkinson’s disease is a degenerative neurological condition that progressively interferes with normal nervous system activity, leading to deterioration in motor and speech capabilities, which makes early identification crucial for prompt medical intervention. In this study, two deep learning approaches were independently applied to handwriting and speech datasets to identify the presence of Parkinson’s disease. Handwriting spiral images were pre-processed using Sobel edge detection to emphasise stroke irregularities caused by shock, and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) was trained to classify the images. Speech recordings were converted to Mel Frequency Cepstral Coefficient (MFCC) spectrograms to reveal vocal characteristics affected by PD. After that, a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model was trained to capture temporal changes. The effectiveness of both models was assessed through accuracy, loss progression, confusion matrix, and receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis. The findings indicate that both CNN and LSTM models can successfully distinguish Parkinson patients from healthy individuals, with gradually increasing accuracy across epochs. The results confirm that handwriting irregularities and speech pattern abnormalities are effective non-invasive digital biomarkers for PD assessment. This research highlights the relevance of deep learning in medical decision support systems for the prompt identification of Parkinson’s disease.

title: Hybrid Model for Enhanced Crypto Currency Forecasting: Polygon (POL) Price Prediction with XGBoost GRU and BiLSTM
authors: A. Kalaivani, V.Asha, J.Sathya, Sahil Rajesh, Ranjita Nigale, Puneeth Kumar
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Expert Clouds and Applications (ICOECA)
abstract: Crypto currency price prediction has become a critical area of financial research because of the rapid growth and changes in the financial market, decentralized structure, and high susceptibility to external factors such as investor sentiment and macroeconomic trends. Existing traditional models are not capable of handling the complex and time-dependent patterns within such data. This research paper proposes a hybrid model which integrates XGBoost, Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), and Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) models to predict the forecasting of crypto currency. The dataset, sourced from the Coin Gecko API, includes 90 days of historical market data enriched with key technical indicators such as SMA-14, SMA-50, and RSI-14. XGBoost was employed for feature-level learning, while GRU and BiLSTM captured temporal dependencies and bidirectional context. Among all models, GRU exhibited the best performance with an RMSE of 0.2060, and the value of MAE with 0.1618, and MAPE with 0.71%, outperforming both XGBoost and BiLSTM.

title: A Parallel Hardware Architecture for the Twiddless Fast Fourier Transform (TFFT)
authors: Rajesh Kumar Mahato, Madhav Prasad Pandey, Sushant Adhikari
publish: 2026 International Conference on ICT and Photonics (ICTP)
abstract: The Twiddless Fast Fourier Transform (TFFT) is a non-interpolated and non-decimated DFT algorithm for integer multiples of a power of two signal lengths. This paper presents a customized and parallel hardware architecture for the TFFT algorithm and compares the computational complexity of the proposed design with direct DFT and radix-2 FFT algorithms. The design have been implemented on a Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) using Verilog and follows the IEEE-754 single-precision floating-point standard. DSP58 primitives have been utilized to perform the required arithmetic operations, and the design is verified through simulation using the Vivado software environment. This paper also evaluates resource utilization and latency for different signal lengths, verifying that while the arithmetic operation requirements are lower than those of the direct DFT, the proposed approach achieves better memory optimization than the radix-2 FFT due to its support for a wider range of valid signal lengths.

title: Energy-Aware Dynamic TDD Scheduling in 5G RAN Using Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Amit Anand, Prabhat Thakur, Valluri Murthy, Priyanka Anand, Neelam Gupta, Ghanshyam Singh
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Advancing Sustainable Solutions through Technologies (ICASST)
abstract: The rapid expansion of 5G networks has increased Radio Access Network (RAN) energy consumption, leading to higher costs and environmental concerns. Static Time Division Duplex (TDD) configurations fail to adapt to fluctuating traffic demands, resulting in spectrum and power wastage. This paper proposes an AI-driven D-TDD framework utilizing Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) within an O-RAN architecture for real-time, energy-aware slot adaptation. The proposed system automatically adjusts uplink and downlink slot ratios, predicts idle transmission opportunities, and activates O-RU smart sleep modes to conserve power while maintaining Quality of Service (QoS). FlexRIC integration, OpenAirInterface (OAI), and USRP B210 hardware testing were employed to validate the framework. Long-term energy savings of up to 65 kWh were observed in realistic macrocell simulations, particularly under low traffic conditions (0–40% load), without compromising throughput. The results demonstrate that AI-driven dynamic TDD scheduling is scalable and robust for energy-efficient 5G and beyond-6G RAN deployments.

title: Reinforcement Learning-Based Bio-Inspired Control for Stability Enhancement in Autonomous Vehicles
authors: T. Venkatamuni, B Pavani Srikavya, S. Bathrinath, S. Gayathri Priya, M Pandikumar, Abhishek Kumar Jain
publish: 2026 Innovations in Machine, Engineering, and Digital Conference (IMED)
abstract: The current paper proposes a support system to increasing the stability of autonomous vehicles by using the combination of reinforcement learning (RL) and bio-inspired control strategy. In particular, we suggest to utilize genetic algorithms to learn optimal control to RL so that the system is able to learn in changing road conditions and disturbances. The genetic algorithm aids in the search of the solution space which seeks the best performing policy to enhance the vehicle stability control. We use the CARLA simulator as an instrument to evaluate and verify the developed approach in a range of driving situations, in sharp turns and slippery roads. The stability aspect of our approach is much better as the response times and change of control was smoother than in the traditional methods. The findings suggest that RL coupled with bio-inspired optimization procedures provides a resilient adaptive solution to obtaining stability improvement in autonomous vehicles, and thus it is suitable to real life situations in autonomous driving systems.

title: AI-Augmented Threat Intelligence Framework for Proactive Vulnerability Assessment in Evolving Enterprise Networks
authors: Kismat Chhillar, Saurabh Shrivastava, Deepak Tomar, Alok Verma
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Advancing Sustainable Solutions through Technologies (ICASST)
abstract: The rapid evolution of cyber threats demands proactive security strategies beyond conventional methods that heavily depend on human expertise and static models. This paper presents an advanced AI-driven threat intelligence framework designed to detect vulnerabilities and improve situational awareness in dynamic enterprise networks. By integrating machine learning, natural language processing, and contextual analytics, the framework automates threat identification, analysis, and response, enhancing detection accuracy while reducing false positives and incident response times. It empowers security teams to anticipate and mitigate emerging vulnerabilities across complex digital infrastructures. Comprehensive architectural and methodological details are provided alongside case studies demonstrating performance improvements across industries. The paper also discusses challenges faced during implementation and proposes strategies to embed ethical considerations and privacy protections. Ultimately, AI’s integration marks a paradigm shift in enterprise cybersecurity, essential for addressing an increasingly hostile digital landscape.

title: NDVI-Driven Forest Depletion Analysis: A Remote Sensing Framework for Evaluating Cement Industry Impacts on Vegetation
authors: T.Poovizhi, S.Christy, Veerasundari.R, G.S.Annie Grace Vimala, Arul Raj A.M, Moorthy Agoramoorthy
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Expert Clouds and Applications (ICOECA)
abstract: Areas where limestone is mined and cement is produced at large scale have undergone much environmental change over the past few years. This paper quantifies the extent of forest loss in the industrial landscapes on the basis of the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) based on the MODIS satellite imagery. Two time periods, pre-2010 (2000-2009) and post-2015 (2016- 2025) periods were contrasted to identify the long term vegetation trends. The NDVI thresholding was used to differentiate between forested and non-forested areas to be able to quantify the changes in the green cover. The interpretation shows that vegetation degradation significantly decreases and most of the major losses have been centered in the areas of cement production centers and mining areas. These results point to the mounting unease between the industrialization and the ecological equilibrium, and the necessity to plan the land-use in a more ecologically conscious way and to approach the long-term management of resources more ecologically responsible.

title: Modeling Adoption Intention of Generative AI Chatbots in Higher Education: A Quantitative Decision-Oriented Analysis
authors: Shubham Puranik, Loveleen Gaur, Ramakrishnan Raman
publish: 2026 Innovations in Machine, Engineering, and Digital Conference (IMED)
abstract: Generative AI (GenAI) chatbots application in educational settings is influenced by measurable decisions and utility features rather than understanding or perceived risk. We propose a measurable adoption intention model utilizing survey data collected from 131 university students. Multiple constructs demonstrating perceived benefits, awareness, concerns, and institutional challenges were analyzed using multiple linear regression. The model explained 30.0% of the variance in adoption intention (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.300, F = 13.47, p < 0.001). Perceived benefits emerged as the dominant predictor (β = 0.428, p < 0.001), while awareness (p = 0.906), concerns (p = 0.574), and challenges (p = 0.103) did not demonstrate self-sufficient statistical significance. The results imply that adoption intention is predominantly utility-driven rather than controlled by familiarity or perceived risk. This study contributes a quantitative, system-level viewpoint on the implementation of GenAI chatbots in higher education.

title: The Limits of Macroscopic Analysis in Global Protest Dynamics: Evaluating Static Clustering vs. Microscopic Complexity
authors: Krishnanand Badu, Sumitra Gyawali, Shailesh Pandey, Nischal Regmi
publish: 2026 International Conference on ICT and Photonics (ICTP)
abstract: In the quantitative analysis of political instability, a fundamental question remains: can global protest dynamics be adequately understood through a macroscopic, static lens, or do they strictly require microscopic, context-specific time series analysis? This study investigates this dichotomy using the Integrated Crisis Early Warning System (ICEWS) dataset (1995–2022). Since protest data is inherently sequential, we argue that standard static methods are insufficient. We employ four clustering methodologies designed to handle temporal data: Standard K-means (baseline), DTW K-means, DFT-based K-means, and ARIMA-based K-means. While internal validation metrics consistently suggest a two-cluster solution, further inspection of the underlying ARIMA parameters reveals that these clusters differ primarily in scale rather than in temporal dynamics. This negative result provides empirical evidence that macroscopic clustering approaches fail to capture the heterogeneity of protest behavior, supporting the necessity of microscopic, country-specific time-series analysis to understand the evolution and drivers of political unrest.

title: Remarks on the Relevance of Privacy Expectations for Default Opt-out Settings
authors: Sebastian Zimmeck
abstract: Over the past few years an increasing number of states in the US have adopted new privacy laws. The majority of these laws require compliance with universal opt-out mechanisms (UOOMs), which allow consumers to send legally binding opt-out signals. However, a number of laws generally do not allow UOOMs to be enabled by default. While some laws exempt privacy-protective software from this prohibition, the exemption does not apply to pre-installed software, e.g., a privacy-protective web browser bundled with an operating system. The reason for not allowing default opt-out settings for pre-installed software is to ensure that settings reflect consumers’ “affirmative, freely given, and unambiguous choice,” as, for example, the Colorado Privacy Act (CPA) is putting it. However, prohibiting vendors of privacy-protective software from turning on UOOMs by default can force them into committing unfair or deceptive acts or practices under the FTC Act and equivalent state laws. Thus, whether UOOMs can be turned on by default on pre-installed software should depend on consumers’ privacy expectations. For pre-installed software that is creating a reasonable expectation for consumers that their privacy will be protected, the simple use of such software should be considered a valid choice for enabling UOOMs. In such software a turned-on UOOM is not a “default setting” but rather the software’s inherent behavior that a consumer expects and chooses through its use. This interpretation of consumer choice is preferable under the CPA and similar laws as it grounds the notice and choice principle in the privacy expectations of consumers and enables companies to compete on better privacy for consumers.
