title: A Selective Reprocessing Mechanism to Achieve End-to-End Data Consistency in Streaming Systems
authors: Felipe Cerezo, Carlos E. Cuesta, Belen Vela
abstract: In this work the challenge of end-to-end data consistency is addressed in the context of an industrial real-time Big Data project, as it cannot be achieved solely by using known tools or architectures. The main goal is to guarantee end-to-end consistency in streaming systems, and, to this end, we propose the design and implementation of a specific-purpose selective reprocessing mechanism. This mechanism involves scanning the real-time data stream and tagging data frames to detect both missing and duplicate information, allowing selective reprocessing of only the affected data. By applying this mechanism to data streaming in the context of our project, we have achieved end-to-end consistency in a short time with significantly low resource usage. It also enables us to know at any moment how far we are from achieving end-to-end consistency. Additionally, we have improved the system’s resilience by adapting the circuit breaker pattern to the streaming process. In conclusion, the selective reprocessing mechanism is effective in ensuring end-to-end data consistency in Big Data streaming projects. Resource usage is optimized, and any potential reprocessing is executed within a short time frame.

title: A Robust Approach for LiDAR-Inertial Odometry Without Sensor-Specific Modeling_supp1-3685966.mp4
authors: Meher Venkata Ramakrishna Malladi
abstract: Accurate odometry is a critical component in a robotic navigation stack, and subsequent modules such as planning and control often rely on an estimate of the robot's motion. LiDARs-based odometry approaches should be robust across sensor types and deployable in different target domains, from solid-state LiDARs mounted on cars in urban-driving scenarios to spinning LiDARs on handheld packages used in unstructured natural environments. In this paper, we propose a robust LiDAR system that does not rely on sensor-specific modeling. Sensor fusion techniques for LiDAR and inertial measurement unit (IMU) data typically integrate IMU data iteratively in a Kalman filter or use pre-integration in a factor graph framework, combined with LiDAR scan matching often exploiting some form of feature extraction. We propose an alternative strategy that only requires a simplified motion model for IMU integration and directly registers LiDAR scans in a scan-to-map approach. Our approach allows us to impose a novel regularization on the LiDAR registration, improving the overall odometry performance. We provide extensive experiments on different datasets covering a wide array of commonly used robotic sensors and platforms. We show that our approach works with the exact same configuration in all these scenarios, demonstrating its robustness. We have open-sourced our implementation so that the community can build further on our work and use it in their navigation stacks.

title: Web Application Performance Engineering Using Gemini AI
authors: Kateryna Babii, Tetiana Afanasieva, Serhii Onishchenko
abstract: Web application performance is a primary determinant of user experience, scalability, and operational cost, yet optimization remains challenging in modern distributed architectures due to dynamic traffic patterns and heterogeneous diagnostic data, including execution logs, query plans, and workload traces. Conventional techniques such as load balancing, caching, and database tuning are effective but typically require extensive manual analysis and expert intervention [1]. This paper investigates the use of Google Gemini as a decision-support component for web application performance engineering. Gemini is not deployed as a runtime controller and does not perform real-time scheduling, resource allocation, or request routing; instead, it is applied during design and testing to analyze workload summaries, slow-query traces, and system logs and to generate actionable optimization recommendations [2]. We evaluate four advisory dimensions: (i) query optimization support, (ii) predictive caching recommendations, (iii) workload analysis for bottleneck identification, and (iv) behavior-aware caching strategies derived from aggregated workload-level user interaction patterns. Using a controlled benchmarking testbed representative of a modern Node.js–PostgreSQL–Redis web stack, we compare a baseline configuration against a Gemini-informed configuration in which model recommendations are implemented and validated by developers under identical workloads. Experimental results show that Gemini-informed optimizations reduce average response latency by up to 2–3×, increase throughput by up to 2× under high concurrency, and improve cache hit ratios to approximately 50% in read-dominant workloads through behavior-informed caching strategies, while maintaining comparable performance under low concurrency. These improvements are achieved without introducing the model into the production control loop. The results demonstrate that large language models can provide practical, explainable, and scalable guidance for web application performance optimization when positioned as advisory systems within established performance engineering workflows. This work presents an experimentally validated integration paradigm in which LLMs augment engineering decision making and complement infrastructure-level optimization techniques rather than replace them. A key distinguishing capability of this paradigm is cross-layer reasoning: by integrating SQL execution plans, application logs, and workload summaries into a unified advisory output, the LLM reduces engineering analysis time by an estimated 85–90% compared to manual performance debugging workflows.

title: A Multifunctional Flexible Patch for All-in-One Flow Field Characterization_supp1-3684157.pdf
authors: Qingshan Wu
abstract: Real-time, multi-parameter monitoring of flow fields within microfluidic channels is critical for biomedical diagnostics and high-throughput analysis. However, conventional sensors are hindered by their bulky size, complex integration, and inability to decouple coupled physical parameters. This work presents a low-cost, flexible carbon electrothermal patch (CEP) fabricated via scalable screen-printing for all-in-one flow field characterization. Through microchannel experiments, the CEP shows a high temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR, 5970 ppm/°C), a high sensitivity (270 ppm per μL/min), and a wide flow-rate response range (from 10 to 1000 μL/min). Meanwhile, based on the relationship among the difference in thermophysical properties of the fluid, the CEP can identify various pure liquids (including glycerol, silicone oil, polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG400), water, and ethanol) and liquids of different concentrations of glycerol-water solutions. Moreover, the CEP functions as a highly sensitive probe for monitoring key dynamic events, including asymmetric flow splitting at a bifurcation, the transit of bubbles with varying sizes, and acoustically driven streaming. These results demonstrate the CEP's potential as a versatile and practical tool for real-time, in-situ diagnostics in advanced microfluidic systems and pave the way for intelligent, feedback-controlled platforms.

title: Exploring Multi-Opposing-Role Play, Narrative-based Simulations, and In-Game Feedback in Cybersecurity Education: Insights from ‘‘The XSS Game’’
authors: Nipuna Hiranya Weeratunge, Rune Hjelsvold
abstract: Cybersecurity education often struggles to engage learners while effectively conveying complex web vulnerabilities. This challenge is particularly evident in topics such as cross-site scripting (XSS), where understanding requires both technical knowledge and an appreciation of attacker, defender, and user perspectives. To address this, The XSS Game (TXG), a web-based multi-opposing-role-playing game, was developed and iteratively refined to support experiential learning of XSS concepts through gameplay. Using a design-based research approach, two versions of TXG were designed and evaluated, incorporating scenario-based tasks, a Simulation Zone, and structured in-game feedback, including confirmatory, explanatory, affirming, and personalized feedback, delivered through a player statboard to support reflection and self-regulation. Evaluation with undergraduate students combined pre- and post-game surveys, in-game behavioral metrics, and exploratory interviews. The study was guided by the Kirkpatrick Model, Kiili’s Experiential Gaming Model, and NASA-TLX to capture learning outcomes, engagement, and perceived cognitive load. Results indicate statistically significant improvements in XSS knowledge (Wilcoxon signed-rank test, p < .001), with a large effect size observed in TXG V2 (r = 0.55). Multi-opposing-role gameplay was rated as supporting learning from multiple perspectives by 82–85% of participants, while engagement with narrative-based simulations increased from 22% in TXG V1 to 71% in TXG V2. Structured in-game feedback was reported to help identify mistakes and improve understanding by 74% of learners. Overall, the findings demonstrate that integrating multi-opposing-role gameplay, narrative-based simulations, and structured feedback can effectively enhance learning, engagement, and motivation in cybersecurity education, providing a reusable framework for educational game design.

title: Navigating in Mixed Traffic: A Human-Centric Approach to Manage Lane Changes of Human-Driven Vehicles
authors: Yongyang Liu, Anye Zhou, Yu Wang, Srinivas Peeta
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: In mixed traffic comprising human-driven vehicles (HDVs) and connected and autonomous vehicles (CAVs), CAV operations often face disruptions from lane changes by HDVs in adjacent lanes. They induce traffic oscillations and unsafe HDV-CAV interactions, potentially undermining HDV drivers’ and CAV users’ mental comfort. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a human-centric control strategy (HCS) to manage HDV lane changes proactively, thereby reducing oscillations, promoting safe HDV-CAV interactions, and mitigating the mental discomfort of HDV drivers and CAV users. The HCS is regulated by an adversarial inverse reinforcement learning-based behavior planner that predicts potential HDV lane changes and plans CAV maneuvers accordingly. For situations without potential lane changes, a proximal policy optimization-based car-following (CF) control model is developed for the CAV to perform smooth CF behavior. For situations with potential lane changes, a lane-change (LC) management model is constructed in a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework and generates a two-level solution to the intricate LC management problem. The upper-level task determines whether to assist or preclude the HDV lane change, and the lower-level task executes the corresponding CAV control actions. Numerical experiments indicate that the HCS manages HDV lane changes effectively to improve traffic smoothness, and reduce HDV drivers’ and CAV users’ mental discomfort across various CAV control setups and HDV driver types. Empirical validation through driving simulator experiments involving 72 participants further illustrates the HCS’s effectiveness in enhancing CAV smoothness, promoting safe and smooth HDV lane changes, and mitigating the mental discomfort of HDV drivers and CAV users.

title: Variable Stiffness Spring Leg With Human-Driven Stiffness Adaptation_supp1-3685968.mp4
authors: David Braun
abstract: The bicycle transmission enables effortless gear shifting to amplify the force produced by human limbs, but it cannot amplify limb power. Conventional variable stiffness springs, placed in series or parallel with human limbs, can amplify both force and power by increasing stiffness. However, amplifying power requires energy proportional to the amount of energy stored in the spring. Here, we present a human-driven robot leg that functions as an energetically conservative variable stiffness spring. Using a pedaling mechanism, the leg stores energy supplied by the human and allows stiffness to be increased before the stored energy is released. This approach amplifies both force and power, analogous to shifting gears while pedaling a bicycle. The prototype confirms force amplification, power amplification via stiffness modulation, and low-cost stiffness adjustment, demonstrating the potential for human-driven robot exoskeletons that employ a pedaling mechanism to enable human energy input and exceed biological force and power limits during energy release.

title: Dual-Population Cooperative Algorithm Integrating an LLM and an Evolutionary Algorithm for AUV Path Planning in Three-Dimensional Marine Environments
authors: Jie Wu, Zhiyong Wang, Liang Cheng, Yanjie Song
abstract: Existing AUV path planning methods still face limitations in modeling real three-dimensional (3D) maritime search and rescue (SAR) environments, multi-objective evaluation and safety constraint expression, and maintaining convergence stability in complex search spaces. To address these issues, we propose a dual-population cooperative algorithm that integrates a large language model (LLM) with an evolutionary algorithm (EA) for marine SAR tasks, named LLM_EA. Firstly, we integrate real seabed topography and 3D ocean currents to construct a unified navigation environment. A multi-objective evaluation framework is further designed to balance energy consumption, safety risk, and attitude stability. During the evolutionary process, LLM_EA performs parallel co-evolution between an EA population for numerical search and an LLM population for semantic reconstruction. A feasibility assurance and cross-population information exchange mechanism is designed to improve feasibility and efficiency, and a topological diversity preservation mechanism is introduced to mitigate topological collapse and maintain structural diversity. Comparative experiments show that LLM_EA consistently generates feasible trajectories under diverse geographical environments and complex constraints while achieving better overall performance and more stable convergence than multiple representative algorithms. Ablation experiments further confirm the contributions of the topological diversity preservation mechanism and the LLM strategy. This framework offers a reusable solution for efficient and robust AUV path planning in real-world 3D maritime SAR scenarios.

title: A Transformer-Enhanced Hybrid Physics-Informed Neural Network for Aircraft Aerodynamic Parameter Identification
authors: Yueji Wang, Yu Hu, Weizhi Chen, Yanshuo Zhang, Shi Yu, Qianchao He, Yunhao Li
abstract: This paper presents a novel hybrid framework that integrates a Transformer encoder and a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) decoder with Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for aircraft aerodynamic parameter identification. Traditional methods, including Genetic Algorithm (GA) and Extended Kalman filter (EKF) techniques, often suffer from difficulties in limited incorporation of physical prior knowledge and precision of aerodynamic parameters in high-fidelity simulator data that closely approximates real flight data. To address these limitations, we propose a hybrid framework that combines the temporal modeling capabilities of Transformers with the physical consistency enforcement of PINNs. The proposed framework employs a Transformer encoder to process historical flight sequences and compress them into a comprehensive contextual representation from which an MLP decoder predict flight trajectories. Furthermore, this architecture is augmented with Fourier feature embeddings, which mitigate the spectral bias commonly observed in traditional MLPs while simultaneously enhancing the representation of multi-frequency dynamics inherent in flight data. To ensure physical consistency throughout the learning process, the aircraft’s longitudinal dynamics equations are embedded as soft constraints within a comprehensive multi-objective loss function. This loss function systematically incorporates multiple critical components, including data fidelity loss, physical loss, initial condition loss, and prior knowledge loss. Finally, an adaptive weighting strategy is implemented to dynamically balance these loss components during the training process, thereby ensuring stable convergence and optimal performance across different flight regimes. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves better precision than MLP-based PINN,GA and EKF baselines, highlighting its potential for high-fidelity aircraft modeling and system identification.

title: Learning from Prototypes: Contrastive Learning with Prior-Aware Multi-Label Chest X-ray Classification
authors: Xuhao Zeng, Haoming Ye, Nanlan Yu, Feng Ding, Keping Yu, Haijun Li, Zhijiang Wan
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Multi-label Chest X-ray (CXR) classification faces significant challenges from the inherently imperfect nature of clinical data, particularly the complex interplay of co-occurring pathologies, training data with a long-tailed distribution, and high visual similarity between distinct diseases. To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework that synergizes medical prior knowledge with prototype-driven contrastive learning, enabling disentangled and discriminative per-pathology representation learning. In particular, our approach integrates a co-occurrence modulated Label Graph Attention (LGA) module, which leverages semantic prior knowledge from a pre-trained large language model (LLM) and statistical co-occurrence patterns from training data to model inter-pathology relationships. Subsequently, a Label-Aware Decoupling (LAD) decoder is proposed to isolate pathology-specific visual features and mitigate feature suppression by dominant classes. Furthermore, we introduce an Adaptive Proto type Contrastive Learning (APCL) mechanism to enhance the discriminability of visually similar pathologies. Extensive experiments on the NIH ChestX-ray14 and CheXpert datasets demonstrate the framework's superiority, achieving state-of-the-art mean AUCs of 0.834 and 0.840, respectively. Furthermore, cross-dataset evaluations on the external MIMIC-CXR dataset validate the framework's exceptional zero-shot and few-shot generalization capabilities, highlighting its strong robustness and potential for real world clinical deployment. The implementation is available at https://github.com/ZengXHYX/Learning-from-Prototypes

title: Design of a High-Contrast Cogging Torque Outer Rotor PM Machine with Continuous Adjustability for Integrated Robot Joint Drive
authors: Ling Qin, Jiyao Wang, Wei Xu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper presents the design methodology of an outer rotor PM machine with continuously adjustable cogging torque for integrated robot joint drive. The proposed machine under normal operation is programmed to have excellent electromagnetic performances as a conventional field modulation permanent magnet (PM) machine, with high electromagnetic torque and low torque ripple that provides smooth power production. Additionally, the proposed machine has a state of high cogging torque, during power-off condition, provides passive locking force to keep the shaft from rolling, which can reduce energy consumption. The cogging torque amplitude can also be adjusted continuously to a desired locking force level. With this design, the mechanical parking/holding brake can be eliminated together with its dust contamination, corrosion and abrasion concerns. This greatly reduces the maintenance need of a system, making it suitable for the application of integrated robot joint system. The evolution of candidate machine structure is shown in this paper, followed by an analytical evaluation of cogging torque generation. Then, the optimization process is employed to the candidate topology to determine the optimal electromagnetic and geometric design parameters. Finally, a prototype based on the optimal topology is accordingly manufactured and experimentally verified. The test result shows a high-contrast cogging torque range of 101% rated torque.

title: A Novel Primary Side Inductor-less Hybrid Phase-Shifted-LLC Converter with Integrated Magnetics
authors: Hang Wang, Hai Nie, Shuanggang Li, Shaoliang An, Ang Shen, Xiangdong Sun, Hongfei Wu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes a novel hybrid phase-shifted-LLC (PSLLC) converter with a planar integrated magnetic core. The converter is composed of an inductor-less phase-shifted full-bridge (IL-PSFB) circuit, whose lagging leg also serves as a half-bridge for an LLC (HB-LLC) resonant circuit. Additional operating modes are found due to the absence of the inductor in traditional PSFB circuit. The soft-switching range of the lagging leg is significantly broadened by optimized inductance design without primary inductor. The principle of proposed hybrid topology is elaborated in detail. The boundary parameter conditions for zero-voltage switching (ZVS) are derived. To enhance power density, an integrated transformer is designed, which integrates two transformers and one inductor into a single magnetic core. A comprehensive optimization model is established to decrease the total loss and the volume of magnetic components. Simulation and experimental results verify the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed hybrid topology converter with the integrated magnetics core.

title: Deep Learning-Based Process Control of Microrobot Swarms Guided by Phase Diagrams_supp6-3686187.mp4
authors: Jiangfan Yu
abstract: Microrobot swarms with locomotion dexterity and shape reconfigurability show immense potential in biomedical applications. Automatic control strategies are critical for the navigation of swarms in unstructured environments. Existing control methods mainly focus on initial and final states of swarms, and swarm process control designed to avoid undesired states throughout the control process is yet investigated. In this work, we develop a deep learning-based process control strategy for swarms guided by phase diagrams. Two deep neural networks are respectively built to model the swarm shape and kinematics. Control approaches based on precise swarm models are designed to automatically tune multiple swarm parameters. A phase diagram-based controller is proposed to guide the swarm reconfiguration while eliminating the coupling effects between swarm parameters. The swarm is enabled to accurately track predefined trajectories while performing continuous reconfiguration with desired states during the entire process. By integrating the process control of swarm pattern and locomotion, the swarm can dynamically adapt to constrained unstructured spaces and achieve robust collision avoidance.

title: Deep Learning-Based Process Control of Microrobot Swarms Guided by Phase Diagrams_supp5-3686187.mp4
authors: Jiangfan Yu
abstract: Microrobot swarms with locomotion dexterity and shape reconfigurability show immense potential in biomedical applications. Automatic control strategies are critical for the navigation of swarms in unstructured environments. Existing control methods mainly focus on initial and final states of swarms, and swarm process control designed to avoid undesired states throughout the control process is yet investigated. In this work, we develop a deep learning-based process control strategy for swarms guided by phase diagrams. Two deep neural networks are respectively built to model the swarm shape and kinematics. Control approaches based on precise swarm models are designed to automatically tune multiple swarm parameters. A phase diagram-based controller is proposed to guide the swarm reconfiguration while eliminating the coupling effects between swarm parameters. The swarm is enabled to accurately track predefined trajectories while performing continuous reconfiguration with desired states during the entire process. By integrating the process control of swarm pattern and locomotion, the swarm can dynamically adapt to constrained unstructured spaces and achieve robust collision avoidance.

title: Performance Enhancement of Drowning Detection Using Synthetic Data
authors: Minju Kim, Jinhwan Koh
abstract: Drowning accidents remain a critical global safety issue, yet vision-based deep learning approaches are hindered by the scarcity of real drowning data due to ethical and legal constraints. This paper proposes a synthetic data–driven drowning detection framework that combines structured prompt-based image generation with a Residual-GAN to construct a balanced dataset of 8,400 synthetic images (400 prompt-generated seeds and 8,000 GAN-generated samples). A lightweight residual CNN (two Residual Blocks with Global Average Pooling) is then trained exclusively on synthetic data. To assess real-world applicability, we perform synthetic-to-real cross-domain validation using an independent test set collected from the Roboflow Universe public dataset platform (500 drowning and 500 non-drowning images) without fine-tuning. The proposed model achieves 98.0% validation accuracy and 94.3% test accuracy, demonstrating performance comparable to AlexNet (94.8%) and ResNet18 (94.9%) while maintaining a significantly more compact architecture. These results indicate that controllable synthetic data generation combined with lightweight residual learning can provide an effective foundation for drowning detection under real-data scarcity.

title: Unfitted Lattice Green’s Function Method for Exterior Scattering in Complex Geometry
authors: Siyuan Wang, Qing Xia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper develops a finite-difference analogue of the boundary integral/element method for the numerical solution of two-dimensional exterior scattering from scatterers of arbitrary shapes. The discrete fundamental solution, known as the lattice Green’s function (LGF), for the Helmholtz equation on an infinite lattice is derived and employed to construct boundary algebraic equations through the discrete potentials framework. Unlike the continuous fundamental solution used in boundary integral methods, the LGF introduces no singularity, which simplifies numerical implementation. Boundary conditions are incorporated through local Lagrange interpolation on unfitted cut cells. The resulting method retains key advantages of boundary integral approaches-including dimension reduction and the absence of artificial boundary conditions–while enabling finite differences for complex geometries. Numerical results demonstrate the accuracy and robustness of the method for various scatterers, including circular, triangular, and multiple-body configurations.

title: A Fast-Transient PLL-Assisted COT Buck Converter with Valley-Clamped Ramp Compensation for High Accuracy
authors: Pang-Jung Liu, Pei-Xuan Ding
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: This paper presents a 0.35-μm CMOS buck converter with valley-clamped internal ramp compensation for ripple-based constant on-time (COT) control. The proposed technique enables stable operation with low-ESR output capacitors while suppressing the dc output offset introduced by conventional compensation methods. To mitigate the transient-response degradation caused by ramp compensation, a transient enhancement circuit is employed to accelerate output-voltage recovery. In addition, a phase-locked loop (PLL) is incorporated to achieve pseudo-fixed-frequency operation in continuous conduction mode (CCM). Experimental results show that the output dc offset is below 3 mV over a load range of 50 mA to 1000 mA. For a 700-mA load step, the recovery times are 1.3 μs and 1.2 μs for step-down and step-up transitions, respectively. Moreover, the switching frequency is regulated at approximately 1 MHz with less than 0.7% variation under load changes from 300 mA to 1000 mA. The prototype achieves FOM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub> values of 5.38/5.83 and FOM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> values of 17.95/19.44 for step-down/step-up operation, demonstrating competitive transient performance and dc accuracy compared with reported switching on-time controlled CMOS buck converters.

title: MFAD: A Multimodal Feature Fusion-Enhanced Time Series Anomaly Detection Framework in Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems
authors: Silin Peng, Yu Han, Lichen Liu, Jinlong Li, Ruonan Li, Zhaoquan Gu, Jie Liu, Xiaowen Chu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems (ICPS) are increasingly vulnerable to sophisticated attacks and operational disturbances that induce subtle and hard-to-detect anomalies, particularly in industrial edge environments. Existing anomaly detection methods often rely on sufficient labeled data and involve excessive computational overhead, hindering real-time detection and lightweight deployment. To address these challenges, we propose a Multimodal Feature fusion-enhanced time series Anomaly Detection framework (MFAD) in ICPS. MFAD enhances the representation of subtle anomalies by jointly modeling temporal dynamics and industrial characteristics through a unified multimodal feature fusion mechanism. Moreover, MFAD adopts a three-stage detection strategy with adaptive thresholding, which further improves robustness under varying operating conditions, while its lightweight overall architecture supports edge deployment. In addition, we provide the Industrial Gas Cyber-Physical System (IGCPS) dataset collected from real-world industrial operations. Experiments on ICPS benchmark datasets of varying scales, including IGCPS, PUMP, WADI, and SWaT, demonstrate that MFAD achieves an F1 score exceeding 96.7% with efficient resource utilization, validating its effectiveness for real-time detection and lightweight deployment in resource-constrained industrial edge environments. Note to Practitioners—This paper is motivated by the increasing need for reliable and efficient anomaly detection in Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems (ICPS), particularly deployed in resource-constrained industrial edge environments. Existing approaches often treat temporal and industrial features separately, rely on sufficient labeled data, and require substantial computational resources, which limits their applicability in real-world industrial settings. In contrast, the proposed MFAD provides a lightweight and practical solution that integrates multimodal feature fusion with robust semi-supervised detection mechanisms to effectively capture subtle anomalies in time series industrial data. The framework is designed with deployment feasibility that it offers strong detection accuracy, low latency, and efficient resource consumption suitable for industrial edge devices. The methods presented here can inform practitioners seeking to enhance the reliability and real-time performance of ICPS anomaly detection systems. Future extensions may focus on expanding MFAD for broader online industrial applications, integrating it with more edge platforms, and enabling large-scale distributed deployment.

title: Receding Horizon Optimization for Network-Balanced Placement of Multiple Stratospheric Airships in Dynamic Environments
authors: Yuanqiao Fan, Xiaolong Deng, Yanchu Yang, Xixiang Yang, Fangchao Bai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Stratospheric airships, as high-altitude platform stations, are potential to provide wide-area wireless communication in next generation networks. This paper addresses the deployment of multiple stratospheric airships in dynamic environments. It investigates the balance between the aggregate rate of user equipment (UEs) and the capacity of inter-airship links, and establishes a formal criterion as a network constraint. A receding horizon optimization framework with heuristic initialization is proposed to jointly optimize placement and airship-to-UE rates. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances throughput and capacity balance compared to Voronoi-based strategies, while maintaining compliance with restricted region constraints under dynamic wind conditions.

title: An Explainable Representation-Level Attention Fusion Network for Smoking Status Prediction
authors: Ramya S S, Prathiba A, Krishnakumar B, Venu K
publish: 2026 Twelfth International Conference on Bio Signals, Images, and Instrumentation (ICBSII)
abstract: Smoking stays as the dominant evitable reasons of worldwide disorders such as cardiovascular, pulmonary, and oncology. Initial detection of smoking habits from systematic medical limits is significant for protective involvement; Despite that, such detection works are complicated by the imbalance of class, non-linear feature contact and restricted interpretability of conventional systems of deep learning. This study put forward a two-stage deep ensemble framework improved with a unique Depiction-Level Attention Fusion Network for exact and interpretable smoking status detection. First of all, a Probabilistic Blending Model is executed with the help of the Echo State Network and a 1D GoogleNet framework as base learners, succeeded by an AlexNet-based stacking meta-learner. Robust scaling, Random Forest feature selection, and Borderline-SMOTE balancing are utilized to enhance selective knowledge. Although the PBM attains a strong detective performance (ROC-AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.855277$</tex>), its decision-making is restricted to probability-level fusion.To tackle this constraint, an attention-based representation fusion mechanism is introduced that merges intermediate embeddings extracted from ESN and GoogleNet before final categorization. The attention layer strongly weighs latent depictions, allowing adaptive feature emphasis and improvised nonlinear modeling. The proposed attention fusion model reaches exceptional performance which is proved by experimental valuation with ROC-AUC of 0.862935, PR-AUC of 0.839491, Accuracy of 0.784633, and the lowest Brier score of 0.146613, outperforming individual base models and the PBM. In addition, a lightweight NLP integration layer allowss risk alerts, hidden smoker identification, and conversational medical interpretation. The proposed framework connects deep learning, ensemble optimization, and clinical explainability, supplying an expandable and illustratable decision-support tool for quick smoking risk detection in systems of healthcare.

title: Non-Invasive Optical Signature Based Hemoglobin Measurement in Neonates
authors: Chitra R, Shanthi Prince
publish: 2026 Twelfth International Conference on Bio Signals, Images, and Instrumentation (ICBSII)
abstract: In neonatal intensive care units (NICUs), hemoglobin (Hb) concentration is measured for several clinical purposes like anemia detection, evaluation of hemolytic diseases and assessment of oxygen delivery. However, in current clinical practice, invasive blood-based methods are used to find <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{H b}$</tex> values. In contrast, this study presents a non-invasive optical signature approach for estimating neonatal hemoglobin concentration using diffuse reflectance spectroscopic system. Spectral data are acquired from the heel of neonates and the relevant wavelength signatures associated with hemoglobin absorption are utilized to estimate Hb values. The proposed method is then clinically validated against reference laboratory values. The correlation value of 0.91 and mean bias of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$- 0.01 \mathbf{g} / \mathbf{d L}$</tex>, with a high proportion (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 6 \%}$</tex>) of samples within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{\pm 1. 0} \mathbf{g} / \mathbf{d L}$</tex> error are observed. These findings indicate that optical signature–based method provides a reliable and non-invasive means of hemoglobin assessment in neonates without any blood prick.

title: Digitalized Smart Trolley for Smart Cities: Enhancing Customer Convenience and Reducing Operational Costs
authors: Suraj Karabadinni, Y Manoj Kumar Reddy, Prem K V, Vasu Naik S M, Vivek Singh, Ajay Kumar Dwivedi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Communication Networks and Computing (CNC)
abstract: As automation and technology explore more applications into the retail space, there is a trend for new innovations in customer service and operational effectiveness. This paper discusses the design and development of a Smart Shopping Trolley based on a Raspberry Pi Pico, which features an RFID-based product scanning, weight checking, and loss prevention. Any product that gets placed into the trolley will be scanned by the embedded EM-18 RFID product reader, and the product will have its relevant details displayed on the LCD screen in real time. The loss prevention is achieved through a load cell-based system, which monitors the trolley's weight in real time and cross compares with the expected weight of the scanned products to determine deviations. User behaviors such as product addition or removal and the generation of bills is managed through a switch panel. After the customer checks out, an immediate digital bill will be displayed, and the system has the capability to alert either the customer or the store administration using the Telegram messaging system to warn for potential items for theft. By providing automatic billing, real-time visible products, and theft prevention, the overall system reduces labor hours for a staff person while reducing the experience time for customers. As presented, the Smart Shopping Trolley illustrates how embedded systems and IoT concepts can meet the needs of modern supermarkets in the 21st century for secure, efficient, and sustainable shopping processes.

title: A Deep Learning Based Framework for Automated Early-Stage Parkinson’s Disease Detection Using Structural Brain MRI
authors: Shrey Shah, Hiren Kavathiya
publish: 2026 Innovations in Machine, Engineering, and Digital Conference (IMED)
abstract: Early detection of Parkinson’s disease (PD) in the prodromal stages is critical for successful intervention, but subtle structural changes in the brain MRI are often not detected by conventional diagnostics. This paper proposes a deep learning-based framework for automatic early-stage PD by using T1-weighted structural brain MRI with a hybrid CNN-Transformer architecture to extract discriminative features from important areas such as substantia nigra and midbrain. The methodology makes use of public datasets such as PPMI and OASIS-3 with preprocessing steps such as skull-stripping, intensity normalization and augmentation. The model, initialized using ImageNet pretrained ResNet-50 and added transformer layers for spatial attention, is 5-fold cross validated with AUC of 96.5%, sensitivity of 94.8%, specificity of 95.2% and F1-score of 94.9% which outperforms models based on traditional ML (e.g. SVM) and CNN-only baselines by 13-16%. Ablation studies validate the transformer’s contribution in learning long range dependencies for asymmetric atrophy, and Grad-CAM heatmaps are useful indicators for visualizing biomarkers such as locus coeruleus changes.

title: From FP32 to QLoRA: A Four-Phase Experimental Pipeline for Assessing Accuracy Recovery in Quantized Transformers
authors: Tanseem Kashyap, Neha Sharma, Raj Gaurang Tiwari, Ambuj Kumar Agarwal, Jay Vasani, Nitin Rakesh
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Expert Clouds and Applications (ICOECA)
abstract: As transformer-based LLMs like BERT, GPT, and their derivatives grow in size and complexity, deployment costs in terms of memory, compute, and inference latency also rise. In order to assess the trade-offs between INT8 quantization, predictive accuracy, and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) techniques, such as LoRA and QLoRA, this work offers a unified empirical methodology. The performance impact of decreasing precision from FP32 to INT8 and the capacity of PEFT algorithms to recover lost accuracy are investigated using a four-phase experimental workflow. BERT-base, ALBERT, DistilBERT, MiniLM, and BERT-mini were tested on SST-2 and MRPC. According to the results, INT8 quantization can significantly reduce accuracy in lightweight models while increasing runtime by up to 70%. For bigger architectures, increasing LoRA rank from 8 to 32 brings accuracy back to within about 5 percentage points of FP32, but highly distilled models are still unstable. Compatibility issues with parameter-shared and customized attention layers were demonstrated by QLoRA. These results demonstrate that universal PEFT techniques are still difficult to implement and that quantization resilience is highly dependent on model architecture.

title: Implantable AI Pacemakers Using On-Device Learning to Prevent Atrial Fibrillation Before Onset
authors: Prakash Gujarati, Divyesh Gohel
publish: 2026 Innovations in Machine, Engineering, and Digital Conference (IMED)
abstract: This paper gives the design and system-level analysis of an implantable artificial intelligence-enabled pacemaker that uses on-device learning to identify and prevent atrial fibrillation before it can impact the patient clinically. The proposed pacemaker transforms lightweight embedded learning models using intracardiac electrograms in real-time to help offer control and decisions to ultra-low-power implantable hardware. Individual adaptation of the patient makes it possible to detect early instances of atrial electrophysiological instability, and preventative pacing with a closed-loop control approach. System analysis indicates that the proposed solution would provide an average lead time of 24 minutes prior to onset with an average prediction accuracy of 87 percent of atrial fibrillation and energy usage that is within clinically acceptable ranges. These findings suggest that anticipatory, learning-pacing can be practiced within the implantable limits and can substantially decrease the atrial fibrillation burden.

title: Next Generation Quantum-IoT Communication Framework for Intelligent and Sustainable Network Solutions
authors: N Sreekanth, M Vijayasanthi, Sayed Sayeed Ahmad, Shaik Taj Mahaboob, Gayathri K M, Thangadurai N
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Advancing Sustainable Solutions through Technologies (ICASST)
abstract: The relationship between quantum computing and the Internet of Things (IoT) is becoming a powerful pathway to develop secure, intelligent and sustainable network systems – however, present-day IoT architectures experience scalability challenges, as well as energy and data vulnerability, due to classical models in cryptography. This paper presents a Next Generation Quantum-IoT Communication Framework (QIoT-CF) that addresses these issues with quantum-based encryption, entanglement-assisted routing, and dynamic sustainability optimization for communication in the framework. The architecture uses Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) to ensure quality data security that is unbreakable, and it uses reinforcement learning–based queuing to balance energy consumption and communication reliability with heterogeneous IoT nodes. The simulations suggest that latency decreased by 45%, energy consumption improved by 43%, and the quantum bit error rate (QBER) improved by 52% relative to current IoT systems. The sustainability controller will change transmission policies based on carbon-aware energy metrics providing an acceptable trade-off between quantum performance and sustainability at-scale. The framework represents a critically significant advancement toward eco-intelligent quantum communication security situated within the 6G vision and beyond.

title: Design and Implementation of an IOT-Enabled Automatic Chemical Pesticide Sprayer Using ESP32 Microcontroller
authors: Sharath M.J, Abhishek V.H, Suman. D, H.S. Vivek, Nagesh. K.N
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Communication Networks and Computing (CNC)
abstract: This project presents the design and implementation of an autonomous robotic system for precision agriculture applications, specifically targeting real-time plant disease detection and targeted pesticide application. The system comprises an ESP32 microcontroller, CP integrated Wi-Fi camera, cloud-based machine learning models, and a multi-tank spraying mechanism to formulate a weather-resistant, field-ready agricultural robot. The autonomous robot will navigate across the crop fields using path-following algorithms on high-resolution remote plant captures and cloud-based AI models that will identify individual diseased plants. Once the robots complete their analysis. If the models pick up on diseased plants during that process. The robot targets pesticides directly at those specific plants. It avoids treating healthy ones unnecessarily. The robot comes equipped with a web-based dashboard for control. This setup offers real-time monitoring of operations. It also provides manual overrides for certain robot actions. All of this works to optimize resource use. It cuts down on chemical waste in the process. Several innovative features stand out in the system. One involves multi-pesticide tank management. This handles different types of diseased plants across the same field. Another is IoT compliance tied to toxicity ratings. It uses real-time connectivity for better oversight. The design ensures compatibility in farm settings. It remains non-intrusive to the surrounding environment. Plus, the robot navigates obstacles effectively. It does so while carrying out the application tasks. The project targets key ongoing issues in modern agriculture. Labor force shortages persist as a major problem. Precision in pesticide application proves essential. Sustainable farming practices demand attention too..

title: Leveraging Ensemble Machine Learning to Evaluate Financial Inclusion and Social Equity: Insights from CatBoost and Gradient Boosting Models
authors: Nithya Sambamoorthy, Inder Pal Singh, Rajalingam Arumuganainar, Shanmuga Pria, Raja Govindan, Sreenivasa Babu Nidamanuri
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Expert Clouds and Applications (ICOECA)
abstract: For long-term economic growth, it is important to have financial inclusion and social fairness. However, it is hard to measure these things because socioeconomic data is complicated and doesn't follow a straight line. This work introduces a machine learning ensemble architecture that integrates the characteristics of the CatBoost and Gradient Boosting models to assess social equality and financial inclusion. A variety of AI-driven and digital transformation indicators are employed to evaluate the institutional and organizational factors that affect inclusion outcomes. These metrics include how well data is used, how well cloud computing is used, how fast decisions are made, how happy customers are, how innovative AI is, how well risks are managed, and how well AI follows ethical guidelines. When training and testing models, standard performance indicators include F1-score, recall, accuracy, and precision. The experimental results show that CatBoost is better at handling categorical data and less likely to overfit than Gradient Boosting on all evaluation metrics. Research comparing different levels of financial inclusion has found that higher levels of digital maturity are linked to happier customers, more innovation, and following ethical AI principles. The findings indicate that ensemble learning methodologies are proficient in evaluating social equity and financial inclusion. These procedures are easy to learn, reliable, and may be used on a large scale. This report gives important information to development agencies, banks, and members of parliament so they can set up data-driven programs that encourage growth for everyone and make it easier for everyone to get financial services in the digital age.

title: Comprehensive Evaluation of Deep Learning Pipelines for Gujarati Sign Language Recognition
authors: Devrajsinh Gohil, Jay Nasit, Om Prakash Suthar
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Communication Networks and Computing (CNC)
abstract: Regional sign languages remain underrepresented in technological solutions, creating significant communication barriers for deaf communities. This paper presents a comprehensive study of three deep learning approaches for Gujarati Sign Language (GSL) recognition, addressing the critical technological gap in regional sign language interpretation. We developed and evaluated three complementary pipelines: a pretrained CNN-SVM baseline achieving 99.7% test accuracy, a MediaPipe-XGBoost landmark-based system achieving 94.03% validation accuracy with optimized inference speed, and a CNN Autoencoder-LightGBM framework for compact deployment. Our experimental evaluation demonstrates that lightweight landmark-driven approaches can achieve competitive accuracy while maintaining real-time performance suitable for practical deployment. The most appropriate pipeline to be used in mobile and embedded applications was the MediaPipe-XGBoost pipeline with the least log loss of 0.3683. This work develops the first full-scale standard for Gujarati sign language recognition and a platform upon which regional sign language technology can be expanded into continuous sign language and multimodal interpretation systems.

title: Multimodal and Transformer-Driven Bone Tumor Classification: State-of-the-Art Review and Mathematical Modeling Framework
authors: Diksha, Bandana Sharma
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Advancing Sustainable Solutions through Technologies (ICASST)
abstract: The development of deep-learning technologies allows examining medical images automatically, although, to date, the existing studies remain disjointed. There are differences in model architecture, combination of multiple data sources, explainability, and clinical validation in studies. In this paper the literature covers 2022 to 2025 recent papers related to CNN-based and transfer learning-based models. In contrast to previous reviews, it has a coherent, single taxonomy of the architectures and constructs a mathematical model that describes how each model derives features, combines other modalities, and how tumors are classified. The comparison reveals that hybrid CNN-Transformer models are always better than the rest, with the highest accuracy of more than 90%. However, there are still challenges: imbalance in datasets, data movement across the domains, and poor explainability. The review provides structural insights on the current trends in the field and indicates future research and efforts necessary to develop AI systems in clinical practice that can be understood and those that are privacy-aware.

title: A Comparative Study on Reaction Times using Visual, Audio, and Haptic Stimuli with Direct, Encoded or Dynamic Deliveries
authors: Fernando Hernandez-Gobertti, Raul Lozano, Ignacio Benito-Frontelo, Jaime Ruiz-Alonso, David Gomez-Barquero
abstract: Reaction times are critical for assessing user response efficacy in applications like virtual environments, assistive technologies, and real-time monitoring. This study examines reaction times across visual, audio, and haptic stimuli, categorized into signal path (direct vs. encoded) and task context (static vs. dynamic), among N = 44 participants. Participants were grouped by demographic factors, including age, gender, and education; and lifestyle factors, including videogame and computer use, and physical exercise. We first analyzed observed reaction times and then derived effective reaction times by subtracting measured processing latencies for each delivery condition. Haptic stimuli yielded the fastest observed responses in the direct and dynamic conditions and comparatively low variability across the full sample. After latency correction, encoded haptic produced the shortest effective reaction times, followed by encoded audio, whereas visual stimuli remained the slowest, particularly in the dynamic condition. Exploratory summaries by demographic and lifestyle subgroup suggested some descriptive differences, but these patterns should be interpreted cautiously because several subgroups were small and imbalanced. Overall, the results support haptic cues as a promising channel for rapid-response applications within the specific end-to-end systems evaluated here, offering valuable insights for designing future technologies.

title: Analysis of Active Damping Methods for Power-Synchronization Dynamics Considering Impacts of Different Inner Loops
authors: Liang Zhao, Xiongfei Wang, Zejie Li
abstract: This article evaluates active damping methods for power-synchronization dynamics in grid-forming (GFM) converters, including frequency-to-active-power (FtP), active-power-to-frequency (PtF), and q-axis-voltage-to-frequency (V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">q</sub>tF) controls. Beyond stability-oriented damping analysis, their impacts on inertial response and damping effectiveness under different inner-loop control structures are systematically evaluated. It is revealed that when inner loops employ virtual-admittance-current control and open-loop voltage control, the PtF and V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">q</sub>tF methods share the same damping mechanism and exhibit equivalent characteristics. In contrast, under vector-voltage-control-based inner loops, a tradeoff emerges: the PtF method provides effective damping but prolongs the inertial-response settling time, whereas the V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">q</sub>tF method has a negligible impact on inertial response but offers limited damping in the low-frequency range. To address this tradeoff, a hybrid damping scheme combining PtF and V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">q</sub>tF is proposed, achieving effective damping while preserving inertial response capability. Simulation and experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: HyperR3SNet: Leveraging Hyperbolic Space and Vision Foundation Models for Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation
authors: Junjie Fu, Chenliang Wang, Mingzhe Liu, Xinghua Li, Yi Liu, Wenjiao Shi, Ruili Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote-sensing semantic segmentation drives by land-use monitoring, urban planning, and ecological assessment, yet progress is hampered by scarce pixel-level labels. To address this issue, we present HyperR3SNet, which is an efficient framework for remote-sensing semantic segmentation that tackles data scarcity and scale variations in overhead imagery. HyperR3SNet transfers self-supervised vision foundation models to remote sensing, providing strong feature generalisation with minimal labeled data. Building on this, a multi-scale cross-attention module is inserted into the backbone, vision transformer layer, enabling the network to extract richer features across widely varying object scales. To keep the model lightweight, a matrix-factorised task head is employed, sharply reducing parameters and computation while sustaining accuracy. HyperR3SNet is the first model to integrate a hyperbolic pixel-level loss with vision foundation model adaptation for cross-domain and low-annotation remote sensing segmentation, leveraging the exponential expansion property of hyperbolic geometry to capture latent inter-class relations and preserve structural consistency under weak supervision. Evaluated on the widely adopted remote sensing segmentation datasets (e.g., iSAID, LoveDA, Potsdam, and Vaihingen), HyperR3SNet achieves mean IoUs of 67.60 %, 55.86 %, 80.07 %, and 96.46 %, respectively, and on average surpasses a broad range of state-of-the-art methods.
